[Over 200 hostages](/wiki/Gaza_war_hostage_crisis "Gaza war hostage crisis") were kidnapped and taken to the Gaza Strip.[[297]](#cite_note-Gillett-306)[[298]](#cite_note-307)[[299]](#cite_note-natureparty-308) Studies modeling trauma exposure and assessing mental health outcomes estimated that approximately 5.3% of Israelis may develop [PTSD](/wiki/PTSD "PTSD"),[[300]](#cite_note-309) with national data showing that probable PTSD nearly doubled from 16.2% to 29.8% and rates of anxiety and depression also rising sharply.[[301]](#cite_note-310)

After clearing militants from its territory, Israel launched [one of the most destructive bombing campaigns in modern history](/wiki/Israeli_bombing_of_the_Gaza_Strip "Israeli bombing of the Gaza Strip")[[302]](#cite_note-311)[[303]](#cite_note-312) and [invaded Gaza](/wiki/Israeli_invasion_of_the_Gaza_Strip "Israeli invasion of the Gaza Strip") on 27 October with the stated objectives of destroying Hamas and freeing hostages.[[304]](#cite_note-313)[[305]](#cite_note-314) The fifth war of the [Gaza–Israel conflict](/wiki/Gaza%E2%80%93Israel_conflict "Gaza–Israel conflict") since 2008, it has been the deadliest for Palestinians in the entire [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict")[[306]](#cite_note-315) and the most significant military engagement in the region since the [Yom Kippur War](/wiki/Yom_Kippur_War "Yom Kippur War") in 1973.[[307]](#cite_note-El_Deeb-2023-316) A [United Nations Special Committee](/wiki/Special_Committee_to_Investigate_Israeli_Practices_Affecting_the_Human_Rights_of_the_Palestinian_People "Special Committee to Investigate Israeli Practices Affecting the Human Rights of the Palestinian People"), [multiple governments](/wiki/South_Africa%27s_genocide_case_against_Israel "South Africa's genocide case against Israel"), and various experts and human rights organisations have concluded that Israel is committing [genocide against the Palestinian people](/wiki/Gaza_genocide "Gaza genocide") due to the harm and loss of life inflicted on civilians during the [Gaza War](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war").[[308]](#cite_note-317)[[309]](#cite_note-ohchr-318)[[310]](#cite_note-319)

In April 2024, Israel initiated a wave of airstrikes on Iran, after Iranian strikes targeted Israel, marking the [2024 Iran–Israel conflict](/wiki/2024_Iran%E2%80%93Israel_conflict "2024 Iran–Israel conflict") the first time in which the two countries have ever directly exchanged fire. In October 2024, Israel [invaded Lebanon](/wiki/2024_Israeli_invasion_of_Lebanon "2024 Israeli invasion of Lebanon")[[311]](#cite_note-320) and exchanged missile barrages with Iran three weeks later, in response of Iranian strikes earlier in that month.[[312]](#cite_note-321) After nearly a year of the [Israel–Hezbollah conflict](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93Hezbollah_conflict_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Israel–Hezbollah conflict (2023–present)") from October 2023 due to Hezbollah shooting rockets at Israel to support Hamas in Gaza, Israel [assassinated](/wiki/2024_Hezbollah_headquarters_strike "2024 Hezbollah headquarters strike") Hezbollah secretary general [Hassan Nasrallah](/wiki/Hassan_Nasrallah "Hassan Nasrallah") in September 2024.[[313]](#cite_note-322) A November 2024 [ceasefire agreement](/wiki/2024_Israel%E2%80%93Lebanon_ceasefire_agreement "2024 Israel–Lebanon ceasefire agreement") instructed Israel to withdraw from Lebanon, which Israel mostly did by February 2025, but against the agreement, Israeli forces stayed in five military outposts on highlands in Southern Lebanon.[[314]](#cite_note-323)[[315]](#cite_note-324) In June 2025, Israel launched a renewed series of airstrikes on Iran, targeting Iran's air defence systems, missile launchers, their military leadership, and their [nuclear programme](/wiki/Nuclear_program_of_Iran "Nuclear program of Iran"), which escalated into a [full-scale war](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Israel_war "Iran–Israel war").[[316]](#cite_note-325)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Satellite_image_of_Israel_in_January_2003.jpg/120px-Satellite_image_of_Israel_in_January_2003.jpg)](/wiki/File:Satellite_image_of_Israel_in_January_2003.jpg)

Israel is located in the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") area of the [Fertile Crescent](/wiki/Fertile_Crescent "Fertile Crescent"). At the [eastern end](/wiki/Eastern_Mediterranean "Eastern Mediterranean") of the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"), it is bounded by Lebanon to the north, Syria to the north-east, Jordan and the West Bank to the east, and Egypt and the Gaza Strip to the south-west. It lies between latitudes [29°](/wiki/29th_parallel_north "29th parallel north") and [34° N](/wiki/34th_parallel_north "34th parallel north"), and longitudes [34°](/wiki/34th_meridian_east "34th meridian east") and [36° E](/wiki/36th_meridian_east "36th meridian east").

The sovereign territory of Israel (according to the demarcation lines of the [1949 Armistice Agreements](/wiki/1949_Armistice_Agreements "1949 Armistice Agreements") and excluding all territories captured by Israel during the 1967 Six-Day War) is approximately 20,770 square kilometres (8,019 sq mi), of which two per cent is water.[[317]](#cite_note-cia-326) However Israel is so narrow (100 km at its widest, compared to 400 km from north to south) that the [exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone "Exclusive economic zone") in the Mediterranean is double the land area of the country.[[318]](#cite_note-327) The total area under Israeli law, including East Jerusalem and the Golan Heights, is 22,072 square kilometres (8,522 sq mi),[[319]](#cite_note-328) and the total area under Israeli control, including the military-controlled and partially Palestinian-governed territory of the West Bank, is 27,799 square kilometres (10,733 sq mi).[[320]](#cite_note-loc-geo-329)

Despite its small size, Israel is home to a variety of geographic features, from the [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev") desert in the south to the inland fertile [Jezreel Valley](/wiki/Jezreel_Valley "Jezreel Valley"), with mountain ranges of the [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee"), [Carmel](/wiki/Mount_Carmel "Mount Carmel") and towards the [Golan](/wiki/Golan_Heights "Golan Heights") in the north. The [Israeli coastal plain](/wiki/Israeli_coastal_plain "Israeli coastal plain") on the shores of the Mediterranean is home to most of the population.[[321]](#cite_note-330) East of the central highlands lies the [Jordan Rift Valley](/wiki/Jordan_Rift_Valley "Jordan Rift Valley"), a small part of the 6,500-kilometre (4,039 mi) [Great Rift Valley](/wiki/Great_Rift_Valley "Great Rift Valley"). The [Jordan River](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River") runs along the Jordan Rift Valley, from [Mount Hermon](/wiki/Mount_Hermon "Mount Hermon") through the [Hulah Valley](/wiki/Hulah_Valley "Hulah Valley") and the [Sea of Galilee](/wiki/Sea_of_Galilee "Sea of Galilee") to the [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea"), the [lowest point](/wiki/Extreme_points_of_Earth "Extreme points of Earth") on the surface of the Earth.[[322]](#cite_note-331) Further south is the [Arabah](/wiki/Arabah "Arabah"), ending with the [Gulf of Eilat](/wiki/Gulf_of_Aqaba "Gulf of Aqaba"), part of the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea"). [Makhtesh](/wiki/Makhtesh "Makhtesh"), or "erosion cirques" are unique to the Negev and the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula"), the largest being the [Makhtesh Ramon](/wiki/Makhtesh_Ramon "Makhtesh Ramon") at 38 km in length.[[323]](#cite_note-332) Israel has the largest number of plant species per square metre of the countries in the Mediterranean Basin[[324]](#cite_note-rinat-333) and contains four terrestrial ecoregions: [Eastern Mediterranean conifer–sclerophyllous–broadleaf forests](/wiki/Eastern_Mediterranean_conifer%E2%80%93sclerophyllous%E2%80%93broadleaf_forests "Eastern Mediterranean conifer–sclerophyllous–broadleaf forests"), [Southern Anatolian montane conifer and deciduous forests](/wiki/Southern_Anatolian_montane_conifer_and_deciduous_forests "Southern Anatolian montane conifer and deciduous forests"), [Arabian Desert](/wiki/Arabian_Desert "Arabian Desert"), and [Mesopotamian shrub desert](/wiki/Mesopotamian_shrub_desert "Mesopotamian shrub desert").[[325]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-334) Forests accounted for 8.5% of the area in 2016, up from 2% in 1948, as the result of a large-scale forest planting programme by the [Jewish National Fund](/wiki/Jewish_National_Fund "Jewish National Fund").[[326]](#cite_note-335)[[327]](#cite_note-336)

### Tectonics and seismicity

Further information: [List of earthquakes in the Levant](/wiki/List_of_earthquakes_in_the_Levant "List of earthquakes in the Levant")

The Jordan Rift Valley is the result of tectonic movements within the [Dead Sea Transform](/wiki/Dead_Sea_Transform "Dead Sea Transform") (DST) fault system. The DST forms the [transform boundary](/wiki/Transform_fault "Transform fault") between the [African Plate](/wiki/African_Plate "African Plate") to the west and the [Arabian Plate](/wiki/Arabian_Plate "Arabian Plate") to the east. The Golan Heights and all of Jordan are part of the Arabian Plate, while the Galilee, West Bank, Coastal Plain, and Negev along with the Sinai Peninsula are on the African Plate. This tectonic disposition leads to a relatively high seismic activity. The entire Jordan Valley segment is thought to have ruptured repeatedly, for instance during the last two major earthquakes along this structure in [749](/wiki/749_Galilee_earthquake "749 Galilee earthquake") and [1033](/wiki/1033_Jordan_Valley_earthquake "1033 Jordan Valley earthquake"). The deficit in [slip](/wiki/Fault_(geology)#Slip,_heave,_throw "Fault (geology)") that has built up since 1033 is sufficient to cause an earthquake of Mw ~7.4.[[328]](#cite_note-Ferry-337)

The most catastrophic known earthquakes occurred in 31 BCE, [363](/wiki/363_Galilee_earthquake "363 Galilee earthquake"), 749, and 1033 CE, that is every ca. 400 years on average.[[329]](#cite_note-MarcoAFTAU-338) Destructive earthquakes strike about every 80 years, leading to serious loss of life .[[330]](#cite_note-IllPreped-339) While stringent construction regulations are in place and recently built structures are earthquake resistant, as of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Israel&action=edit) many public buildings as well as 50,000 residential buildings did not meet the new standards and were "expected to collapse" if exposed to a strong earthquake.[[330]](#cite_note-IllPreped-339)

### Climate

Further information: [Climate change in Israel](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Israel "Climate change in Israel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Soil_moisture_and_climate_change.svg/500px-Soil_moisture_and_climate_change.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Soil_moisture_and_climate_change.svg)

The projections of the [IPCC Sixth Assessment Report](/wiki/IPCC_Sixth_Assessment_Report "IPCC Sixth Assessment Report") show clearly the impacts of climate change on Israel even at 2 degrees of warming.

Temperatures vary widely, especially during the winter. Coastal areas, such as those of Tel Aviv and Haifa, have a typical [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") with cool, rainy winters and long, hot summers. The area of Beersheba and the northern Negev have a [semi-arid climate](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") with hot summers, cool winters, and fewer rainy days. The southern Negev and the Arabah areas have a [desert climate](/wiki/Desert_climate "Desert climate") with very hot, dry summers, and mild winters with few days of rain. The highest temperature of 54 °C (129 °F) was recorded in 1942 in the [Tirat Zvi](/wiki/Tirat_Zvi "Tirat Zvi") kibbutz.[[331]](#cite_note-watzman-340)[[332]](#cite_note-341) Mountainous regions can be windy and cold, and areas at elevation of 750 metres (2,460 ft) or more (same elevation as Jerusalem) usually receive at least one [snowfall](/wiki/Snow_in_Israel "Snow in Israel") each year.[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldreich200385-342) From May to September, rain is rare.[[334]](#cite_note-343)[[335]](#cite_note-344)

There are four different [phytogeographic](/wiki/Phytogeography "Phytogeography") regions, due to its location between the temperate and tropical zones. For this reason, the flora and fauna are extremely diverse. There are 2,867 known [species of plants in Israel](/wiki/List_of_endemic_flora_of_Israel "List of endemic flora of Israel"). Of these, at least 253 species are [introduced and non-native](/wiki/List_of_adventive_wild_plants_in_Israel "List of adventive wild plants in Israel").[[336]](#cite_note-345) There are 380 [Israeli nature reserves](/wiki/National_parks_and_nature_reserves_of_Israel "National parks and nature reserves of Israel").[[337]](#cite_note-346)

With scarce water resources, Israel has developed various water-saving technologies, including [drip irrigation](/wiki/Drip_irrigation "Drip irrigation").[[338]](#cite_note-347)[[339]](#cite_note-348) The considerable sunlight available for [solar energy](/wiki/Solar_energy "Solar energy") makes [Israel the leading nation in solar energy](/wiki/Solar_power_in_Israel "Solar power in Israel") use per capita—practically every house uses solar panels for water heating.[[340]](#cite_note-Solar_energy-349) The [Ministry of Environmental Protection](/wiki/Ministry_of_Environmental_Protection_(Israel) "Ministry of Environmental Protection (Israel)") has reported that [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Israel "Climate change in Israel") "will have a decisive impact on all areas of life", particularly for vulnerable populations.[[341]](#cite_note-MEP_2020-350)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Israeli system of government](/wiki/Israeli_system_of_government "Israeli system of government") and [Politics of Israel](/wiki/Politics_of_Israel "Politics of Israel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Isaac_Herzog%2C_July_2021_%28D1233-049%29.JPG/250px-Isaac_Herzog%2C_July_2021_%28D1233-049%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Isaac_Herzog,_July_2021_(D1233-049).JPG)

[Isaac Herzog](/wiki/Isaac_Herzog "Isaac Herzog")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_Israel "President of Israel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Benjamin_Netanyahu%2C_February_2023.jpg/250px-Benjamin_Netanyahu%2C_February_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Benjamin_Netanyahu,_February_2023.jpg)

[Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Israel "Prime Minister of Israel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/PikiWiki_Israel_7260_Knesset-Room.jpg/250px-PikiWiki_Israel_7260_Knesset-Room.jpg)](/wiki/File:PikiWiki_Israel_7260_Knesset-Room.jpg)

The [Knesset](/wiki/Knesset "Knesset") chamber, home to the Israeli parliament

Israel has a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system"), [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") and [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage"). A member of parliament supported by a parliamentary majority becomes the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Israel "Prime Minister of Israel")—usually this is the chair of the largest party. The prime minister is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") and of [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Israel "Cabinet of Israel").[[342]](#cite_note-cia2-351)[[343]](#cite_note-352) The [president](/wiki/President_of_Israel "President of Israel") is [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"), with largely ceremonial duties.[[342]](#cite_note-cia2-351)

Israel is governed by a 120-member parliament, known as the [Knesset](/wiki/Knesset "Knesset"). Membership of the Knesset is based on proportional representation of [political parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Israel "List of political parties in Israel"),[[344]](#cite_note-353)[[345]](#cite_note-354) with a 3.25% electoral threshold, which in practice has resulted in coalition governments. Residents of Israeli settlements in the West Bank are eligible to vote,[[346]](#cite_note-355) and after the [2015 election](/wiki/2015_Israeli_legislative_election "2015 Israeli legislative election"), 10 of the 120 members of the Knesset (8%) were settlers.[[347]](#cite_note-356) Parliamentary [elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Israel "Elections in Israel") are scheduled every four years, but unstable coalitions or a [no-confidence vote](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence") can dissolve a government earlier.[[46]](#cite_note-cnn-54) The first Arab-led party was established in 1988,[[348]](#cite_note-357) and as of 2022, Arab-led parties hold about 10% of seats.[[349]](#cite_note-358) A party cannot run for election to the Knesset if its objectives or actions include the "negation of the existence of the State of Israel as the state of the Jewish people".

The [Basic Laws of Israel](/wiki/Basic_Laws_of_Israel "Basic Laws of Israel") function as an [uncodified constitution](/wiki/Uncodified_constitution "Uncodified constitution"). These define Israel as a [Jewish and democratic state](/wiki/Jewish_and_democratic_state "Jewish and democratic state") and the nation-state of exclusively the Jewish people.[[350]](#cite_note-freedomhouse2008-359) In 2003, the Knesset began to draft an official constitution based on these laws.[[317]](#cite_note-cia-326)[[351]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMazie200634-360)

Israel has no official religion,[[352]](#cite_note-361)[[353]](#cite_note-362)[[354]](#cite_note-363) but the definition of the state as "Jewish and democratic" creates a strong connection with [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"). On 19 July 2018, the Knesset passed a Basic Law that characterises Israel as principally a "[Nation State of the Jewish People](/wiki/Basic_Law:_Israel_as_the_Nation-State_of_the_Jewish_People "Basic Law: Israel as the Nation-State of the Jewish People")" and Hebrew as its official language. The bill ascribes an undefined "special status" to the Arabic language.[[355]](#cite_note-364) The same bill gives Jews a unique right to national self-determination and views the developing of Jewish settlement in the country as "a national interest", empowering the government to "take steps to encourage, advance and implement this interest".[[356]](#cite_note-365)

The State of Israel is divided into six main administrative districts, known as *mehozot* (Hebrew: מחוזות; sg.: *mahoz*)—[Center](/wiki/Central_District_(Israel) "Central District (Israel)"), [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa_District "Haifa District"), [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem_District "Jerusalem District"), [North](/wiki/Northern_District_(Israel) "Northern District (Israel)"), [South](/wiki/Southern_District_(Israel) "Southern District (Israel)"), and [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_District "Tel Aviv District"), as well as the [Judea and Samaria Area](/wiki/Judea_and_Samaria_Area "Judea and Samaria Area") in the West Bank. All of the Judea and Samaria Area and parts of the Jerusalem and Northern districts are not recognised internationally as part of Israel. Districts are divided into 15 sub-districts known as *nafot* (Hebrew: נפות; sg.: *nafa*), which are partitioned into 50 natural regions.[[357]](#cite_note-366)

### Israeli citizenship law

Main article: [Israeli citizenship law](/wiki/Israeli_citizenship_law "Israeli citizenship law")

The 1950 [Law of Return](/wiki/Law_of_Return "Law of Return") grants Jews the unrestricted [right to immigrate](/wiki/Jus_soli#Restricted_jus_soli "Jus soli") to Israel and obtain Israeli citizenship. Individuals born within the country receive [birthright citizenship](/wiki/Jus_sanguinis "Jus sanguinis") if at least one parent is a citizen.[[360]](#cite_note-369) Israeli law defines Jewish nationality as distinct from Israeli nationality, and the [Supreme Court of Israel](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Israel "Supreme Court of Israel") has ruled that an Israeli nationality does not exist.[[361]](#cite_note-tekiner-370)[[362]](#cite_note-371) A Jewish national is defined as any person practicing Judaism and their descendants.[[361]](#cite_note-tekiner-370)
