[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Map_of_Israel%2C_neighbours_and_occupied_territories-en.svg/500px-Map_of_Israel%2C_neighbours_and_occupied_territories-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Israel,_neighbours_and_occupied_territories-en.svg)

Map of Israel showing the West Bank, the Gaza Strip, and the Golan Heights

In 1967, as a result of the Six-Day War, Israel captured and occupied the West Bank (including East Jerusalem), the Gaza Strip and the Golan Heights. Israel also captured the Sinai Peninsula but returned it to Egypt as part of the 1979 Egypt–Israel peace treaty.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBregman2002186–187-257) Between 1982 and 2000, Israel occupied [part of southern Lebanon](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_Southern_Lebanon "Israeli occupation of Southern Lebanon"), in what was known as the [Security Belt](/wiki/South_Lebanon_security_belt_administration "South Lebanon security belt administration"). Since capture of these territories, Israeli settlements and military installations have been built within each of them, except Lebanon.

The Golan Heights and East Jerusalem have been fully incorporated under Israeli law but not under international law. Israel has applied civilian law to both areas and granted their inhabitants permanent residency status and the ability to apply for citizenship. The UN Security Council has declared the annexation of the Golan Heights and East Jerusalem to be "null and void" and continues to view the territories as occupied.[[363]](#cite_note-372)[[364]](#cite_note-373) The status of East Jerusalem in any future peace settlement has at times been a difficult issue in negotiations between Israeli governments and representatives of the Palestinians.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/West_Bank_Fence_South_Hebron.JPG/250px-West_Bank_Fence_South_Hebron.JPG)](/wiki/File:West_Bank_Fence_South_Hebron.JPG)

The [Israeli West Bank barrier](/wiki/Israeli_West_Bank_barrier "Israeli West Bank barrier") is a separation barrier built by Israel along the Green Line and inside parts of the West Bank.

The West Bank area, excluding East Jerusalem, is known as [the Judea and Samaria Area.](/wiki/Judea_and_Samaria_Area "Judea and Samaria Area")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]The almost 400,000 Israeli settlers residing in the area are considered part of Israel's population, have Knesset representation, are subject to a [large part of Israel's civil and criminal laws](/wiki/Enclave_law "Enclave law"), and their output is considered part of Israel's economy.[[365]](#cite_note-Sher-374)[[fn 4]](#cite_note-oecd-24) The land is not considered part of Israel under Israeli law, as Israel has consciously refrained from annexing the territory, without ever relinquishing its legal claim to the land or defining a border.[[365]](#cite_note-Sher-374) Israeli political opposition to annexation primarily stems from the perceived "demographic threat" of incorporating the West Bank's Palestinian population into Israel.[[365]](#cite_note-Sher-374) Outside of the Israeli settlements, the West Bank remains under direct Israeli military rule, and Palestinians in the area cannot become Israeli citizens.

The international community maintains that Israel does not have sovereignty in the West Bank and considers Israel's control of the area to be the longest military occupation in modern history.[[368]](#cite_note-occhist-377) The West Bank was occupied and annexed by Jordan in 1950, following the 1949 Armistice Agreements. Only Britain recognised this annexation, and Jordan has since [ceded its claim](/wiki/Jordanian_disengagement_from_the_West_Bank "Jordanian disengagement from the West Bank") to the territory to the PLO. The population is mainly Palestinians, including refugees of the 1948 Arab–Israeli War.[[369]](#cite_note-378) From their occupation in 1967 until 1993, the Palestinians living in these territories were under [Israeli military administration](/wiki/Coordinator_of_Government_Activities_in_the_Territories "Coordinator of Government Activities in the Territories"). Since the [Israel–PLO letters of recognition](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93Palestine_Liberation_Organization_letters_of_recognition "Israel–Palestine Liberation Organization letters of recognition"), most of the Palestinian population and [cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_administered_by_the_State_of_Palestine "List of cities administered by the State of Palestine") have been under the internal jurisdiction of the Palestinian Authority, and only partial Israeli military control, although Israel has redeployed its troops and reinstated full military administration during periods of unrest. Israel's claim of universal suffrage has been questioned due to its blurred territorial boundaries, its simultaneous extension of voting rights to Israeli settlers in the occupied territories and denial of voting rights to their Palestinian neighbours, as well as the alleged [ethnocratic](/wiki/Ethnocracy "Ethnocracy") nature of the state.[[370]](#cite_note-379)[[371]](#cite_note-380)

The Gaza Strip is considered to be a "foreign territory" under Israeli law. Israel and Egypt operate a land, air, and sea [blockade of the Gaza Strip](/wiki/Blockade_of_the_Gaza_Strip "Blockade of the Gaza Strip"). The Gaza Strip was occupied by Israel after 1967. In 2005, as part of a [unilateral disengagement plan](/wiki/Israel%27s_unilateral_disengagement_plan "Israel's unilateral disengagement plan"), Israel removed its settlers and forces from the territory but continues to maintain control of its airspace and waters. The international community, including numerous international humanitarian organisations and UN bodies, consider Gaza to remain occupied.[[372]](#cite_note-381)[[373]](#cite_note-382)[[374]](#cite_note-383)[[375]](#cite_note-384)[[376]](#cite_note-385) Following the 2007 Battle of Gaza, when [Hamas assumed power in the Gaza Strip](/wiki/Hamas_government_in_the_Gaza_Strip "Hamas government in the Gaza Strip"),[[377]](#cite_note-gaza-386) Israel tightened control of the Gaza crossings along [its border](/wiki/Gaza%E2%80%93Israel_barrier "Gaza–Israel barrier"), as well as by sea and air, and prevented persons from entering and exiting except for isolated cases it deemed humanitarian.[[377]](#cite_note-gaza-386) Gaza has a [border with Egypt](/wiki/Egypt%E2%80%93Gaza_border "Egypt–Gaza border"), and an agreement between Israel, the EU, and the PA governs how border crossings take place.[[378]](#cite_note-387) The application of democracy to its Palestinian citizens and the selective application of Israeli democracy in the Israeli-controlled Palestinian territories have been criticised.[[379]](#cite_note-Slater2020-388)[[380]](#cite_note-White2012-389)

#### International opinion

See also: [Israeli war crimes](/wiki/Israeli_war_crimes "Israeli war crimes")

The [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice") said, in its [2004 advisory opinion](/wiki/International_law_and_the_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_conflict#2004_advisory_opinion_of_the_ICJ "International law and the Arab–Israeli conflict") on the legality of the construction of the West Bank barrier, that the lands captured by Israel in the Six-Day War, including East Jerusalem, are occupied territory and found that the construction of the wall within the occupied Palestinian territory violates international law.[[381]](#cite_note-390) Most negotiations relating to the territories have been on the basis of [UN Security Council Resolution 242](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_242 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 242"), which emphasises "the inadmissibility of the acquisition of territory by war", and calls on Israel to withdraw from occupied territories in return for normalisation of relations with Arab states ("[Land for peace](/wiki/Land_for_peace "Land for peace")").[[382]](#cite_note-391)[[383]](#cite_note-392)[[384]](#cite_note-393) Israel has been criticised for engaging in systematic and widespread violations of [human rights in the occupied territories](/wiki/Human_rights_in_the_Israeli-occupied_territories "Human rights in the Israeli-occupied territories"), including occupation[[385]](#cite_note-394) and war crimes against civilians.[[386]](#cite_note-tny1-395)[[387]](#cite_note-396)[[388]](#cite_note-397)[[389]](#cite_note-398) The allegations include violations of international humanitarian law[[390]](#cite_note-399) by the [UN Human Rights Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Human_Rights_Council "United Nations Human Rights Council").[[391]](#cite_note-400) The [U.S. State Department](/wiki/U.S._State_Department "U.S. State Department") has called reports of [abuses of significant human rights of Palestinians](/wiki/Human_rights_violations_against_Palestinians_by_Israel "Human rights violations against Palestinians by Israel") "credible" both within Israel[[392]](#cite_note-401) and the occupied territories.[[393]](#cite_note-402) [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") and other NGOs have documented mass arbitrary arrests, torture, unlawful killings, systemic abuses and impunity[[394]](#cite_note-403)[[395]](#cite_note-404)[[396]](#cite_note-405)[[397]](#cite_note-406) in tandem with a denial of the right to [Palestinian self-determination](/wiki/Palestinian_self-determination "Palestinian self-determination").[[398]](#cite_note-407)[[399]](#cite_note-408)[[400]](#cite_note-409)[[401]](#cite_note-410)[[402]](#cite_note-411) Prime Minister Netanyahu has defended the country's security forces for protecting the innocent from terrorists[[403]](#cite_note-412) and expressed contempt for what he describes as a lack of concern about the human rights violations committed by "criminal killers".[[404]](#cite_note-413)

The [international community](/wiki/International_community "International community") widely regards Israeli settlements in the occupied territories [illegal under international law](/wiki/International_law_and_Israeli_settlements "International law and Israeli settlements").[[405]](#cite_note-414) [United Nations Security Council Resolution 2334](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_2334 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 2334") (passed 2016) states that Israel's settlement activity constitutes a "flagrant violation" of [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law") and demands that Israel stop such activity and fulfill its obligations as an [occupying power](/wiki/Military_occupation#The_occupying_power "Military occupation") under the [Fourth Geneva Convention](/wiki/Fourth_Geneva_Convention "Fourth Geneva Convention").[[406]](#cite_note-toi-415) A [United Nations special rapporteur](/wiki/United_Nations_special_rapporteur "United Nations special rapporteur") concluded that the settlement programme was a war crime under the [Rome Statute](/wiki/Rome_Statute "Rome Statute"),[[407]](#cite_note-416) and [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") found that the settlement programme constitutes an illegal transfer of civilians into occupied territory and "pillage", which is prohibited by the [Hague Conventions](/wiki/Hague_Conventions_of_1899_and_1907 "Hague Conventions of 1899 and 1907") and [Geneva Conventions](/wiki/Geneva_Conventions "Geneva Conventions") as well as being a war crime under the Rome Statute.[[408]](#cite_note-417)

In a [2024 advisory opinion](/wiki/International_law_and_the_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_conflict#2024_advisory_opinion_of_the_ICJ "International law and the Arab–Israeli conflict"), the International Court of Justice stated that occupation of the Palestinian territories violated international law; Israel should end its occupation as quickly as possible and pay reparations. In addition, the court found that Israel was in breach of article 3 of the [International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination](/wiki/International_Convention_on_the_Elimination_of_All_Forms_of_Racial_Discrimination "International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination"), which requires states to prevent, prohibit and eradicate all practices of racial segregation and apartheid.[[409]](#cite_note-418)[[410]](#cite_note-419)[[411]](#cite_note-420)

#### Accusations of apartheid

Main article: [Israeli apartheid](/wiki/Israeli_apartheid "Israeli apartheid")

Treatment of Palestinians within the occupied territories and to a lesser extent in Israel itself have drawn widespread accusations that it is guilty of [apartheid](/wiki/Crime_of_apartheid "Crime of apartheid"), a [crime against humanity](/wiki/Crime_against_humanity "Crime against humanity") under the Rome Statute and the [International Convention on the Suppression and Punishment of the Crime of Apartheid](/wiki/International_Convention_on_the_Suppression_and_Punishment_of_the_Crime_of_Apartheid "International Convention on the Suppression and Punishment of the Crime of Apartheid").[[412]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShakir2021-421)[[413]](#cite_note-ATC-422) *The Washington Post'*s 2021 survey of scholars and academic experts on the Middle East found an increase from 59% to 65% of these scholars describing Israel as a "one-state reality akin to apartheid".[[414]](#cite_note-423)[[415]](#cite_note-424) The claim that Israel's policies for Palestinians [within Israel](/wiki/Palestinian_citizens_of_Israel "Palestinian citizens of Israel") or [within Israeli-occupied territories](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories") amount to apartheid has been affirmed by Israeli human rights organisation [B'tselem](/wiki/B%27tselem "B'tselem"),[[416]](#cite_note-425)[[417]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEB'Tselem2021-426) University Network for Human Rights,[[418]](#cite_note-427) and international human rights organisations such as Amnesty International[[419]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAmnesty_International2022-428) and [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch").[[413]](#cite_note-ATC-422)[[420]](#cite_note-Arno-429) Israeli human rights organisation [Yesh Din](/wiki/Yesh_Din "Yesh Din") has also accused Israel of apartheid.[[420]](#cite_note-Arno-429) Amnesty's claim was criticised by politicians and representatives from Israel and its closest allies such as, the US,[[421]](#cite_note-430) the UK,[[422]](#cite_note-431) the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"),[[423]](#cite_note-432) Australia,[[424]](#cite_note-433) Netherlands[[425]](#cite_note-434) and Germany,[[426]](#cite_note-435) while said accusations were welcomed by Palestinians[[427]](#cite_note-436) and the [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League").[[428]](#cite_note-437) In 2022, Michael Lynk, a Canadian law professor [appointed by the U.N. Human Rights Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Special_Rapporteur_on_the_occupied_Palestinian_territories "United Nations Special Rapporteur on the occupied Palestinian territories") said that the situation met the legal definition of apartheid, and concluded: "Israel has imposed upon Palestine an apartheid reality in a post-apartheid world".[[429]](#cite_note-438)[[430]](#cite_note-439) Subsequent reports from his successor, [Francesca Albanese](/wiki/Francesca_Albanese "Francesca Albanese") and from [Permanent United Nations Fact Finding Mission on the Israel Palestine conflict](/wiki/Permanent_United_Nations_Fact_Finding_Mission_on_the_Israel_Palestine_conflict "Permanent United Nations Fact Finding Mission on the Israel Palestine conflict") chair Navi Pillay echoed the opinion.[[431]](#cite_note-440)[[432]](#cite_note-441)

In February 2024, the [ICJ held public hearings](/wiki/ICJ_case_on_Israel%27s_occupation_of_the_Palestinian_territories "ICJ case on Israel's occupation of the Palestinian territories") in regards to the legal consequences arising from the policies and practices of Israel in the occupied Palestinian territory including East Jerusalem. During the hearings, 24 states and three international organisations said that Israeli practices amount to a breach of the prohibition of apartheid and/or amount to prohibited acts of racial discrimination.[[433]](#cite_note-442) The [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice") in its [2024 advisory opinion](/wiki/ICJ_case_on_Israel%27s_occupation_of_the_Palestinian_territories "ICJ case on Israel's occupation of the Palestinian territories") found that Israel's occupation of the Palestinian territories constitutes systemic discrimination and is in breach of Article 3 of the [International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination](/wiki/International_Convention_on_the_Elimination_of_All_Forms_of_Racial_Discrimination "International Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination"), which prohibits racial segregation and apartheid. The opinion is silent as to whether the discrimination amounts to apartheid; individual judges were split on the question.[[434]](#cite_note-443)[[435]](#cite_note-444)[[436]](#cite_note-445)[[437]](#cite_note-446)[[438]](#cite_note-auto7-447)[[439]](#cite_note-auto8-448)

### Foreign relations

Main articles: [Foreign relations of Israel](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Israel "Foreign relations of Israel") and [International recognition of Israel](/wiki/International_recognition_of_Israel "International recognition of Israel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Countries_recognizing_Israel.svg/500px-Countries_recognizing_Israel.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Countries_recognizing_Israel.svg)

State of Israel

Countries that recognise Israel

Countries that have withdrawn their recognition of Israel

Countries that have suspended/cut bilateral ties, but maintain recognition

Countries that have never recognised Israel

Israel maintains diplomatic relations with 165 [UN member states](/wiki/United_Nations_member_states "United Nations member states"), the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), the [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands "Cook Islands") and [Niue](/wiki/Niue "Niue"). It has 107 [diplomatic missions](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_of_Israel "List of diplomatic missions of Israel");[[440]](#cite_note-449) countries with which it has no diplomatic relations include most Muslim countries.[[441]](#cite_note-450) Six out of 22 nations in the [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League") have normalised relations with Israel. Israel remains formally in a [state of war with Syria](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93Syria_relations "Israel–Syria relations"), dating back uninterrupted to 1948. It has been in a similarly [formal state of war with Lebanon](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93Lebanon_relations "Israel–Lebanon relations") since the end of the [Lebanese Civil War](/wiki/Lebanese_Civil_War "Lebanese Civil War") in 2000, with the Israel–Lebanon border remaining unagreed by treaty.

Despite the peace treaty between Israel and Egypt, Israel is still widely considered an enemy country among Egyptians.[[442]](#cite_note-451) Iran withdrew its recognition of Israel during the [Islamic Revolution](/wiki/Islamic_Revolution "Islamic Revolution").[[443]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbadi200447–49-452) Israeli citizens may not visit Syria, Lebanon, Iraq, Saudi Arabia, and Yemen without permission from the [Ministry of the Interior](/wiki/Ministry_of_Interior_(Israel) "Ministry of Interior (Israel)").[[444]](#cite_note-453) As a result of the 2008–09 Gaza War, Mauritania, Qatar, Bolivia, and Venezuela suspended political and economic ties with Israel,[[445]](#cite_note-al-jaz-eng-454) though Bolivia renewed ties in 2019.[[446]](#cite_note-455)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Bill_Clinton%2C_Yitzhak_Rabin%2C_Yasser_Arafat_at_the_White_House_1993-09-13.jpg/250px-Bill_Clinton%2C_Yitzhak_Rabin%2C_Yasser_Arafat_at_the_White_House_1993-09-13.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bill_Clinton,_Yitzhak_Rabin,_Yasser_Arafat_at_the_White_House_1993-09-13.jpg)

Yitzhak Rabin and Yasser Arafat at the signing ceremony of the [Oslo Accords peace agreements](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords"), with then US President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") (center)

The [United States](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Israel–United States relations") and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93Russia_relations "Israel–Russia relations") were the first two countries to recognise the State of Israel, having declared recognition roughly simultaneously.[[447]](#cite_note-456) Diplomatic relations with the Soviet Union were broken in 1967 following the Six-Day War and renewed in 1991.[[448]](#cite_note-457) The United States regards Israel as its "most reliable partner in the Middle East",[[449]](#cite_note-458) based on "common democratic values, religious affinities, and security interests".[[450]](#cite_note-459) The US has provided $68 billion [in military assistance](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93United_States_military_relations "Israel–United States military relations") and $32 billion in grants to Israel since 1967, under the [Foreign Assistance Act](/wiki/Foreign_Assistance_Act "Foreign Assistance Act") (period beginning 1962),[[451]](#cite_note-PNADR900-460) more than any other country for that period until 2003.[[451]](#cite_note-PNADR900-460)[[452]](#cite_note-461)[[453]](#cite_note-462) Most surveyed Americans have held consistently favourable views of Israel.[[454]](#cite_note-463)[[455]](#cite_note-464) The United Kingdom is seen as having a "natural" [relationship](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93United_Kingdom_relations "Israel–United Kingdom relations") with Israel because of the Mandate for Palestine.[[456]](#cite_note-465) By 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Israel&action=edit), [Germany](/wiki/Germany%E2%80%93Israel_relations "Germany–Israel relations") had paid 25 billion euros in [reparations to Israel](/wiki/Reparations_Agreement_between_Israel_and_West_Germany "Reparations Agreement between Israel and West Germany") and individual Israeli Holocaust survivors.[[457]](#cite_note-466) Israel [is included](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93European_Union_relations "Israel–European Union relations") in the European Union's [European Neighbourhood Policy](/wiki/European_Neighbourhood_Policy "European Neighbourhood Policy").[[458]](#cite_note-467)

Although [Turkey and Israel](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93Turkey_relations "Israel–Turkey relations") did not establish full diplomatic relations until 1991,[[459]](#cite_note-468) Turkey has cooperated with the Jewish state since its recognition of Israel in 1949. Turkey's ties to other Muslim-majority nations in the region have at times resulted in pressure from Arab and Muslim states to temper its relationship with Israel.[[460]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbadi20044–6-469) Relations took a downturn after the 2008–09 Gaza War and Israel's [raid of the Gaza flotilla](/wiki/2010_Gaza_flotilla_raid "2010 Gaza flotilla raid").[[461]](#cite_note-470) Relations between [Greece and Israel](/wiki/Greece%E2%80%93Israel_relations "Greece–Israel relations") have improved since 1995 after decline of Israeli–Turkish relations.[[462]](#cite_note-471) The two countries have a defence cooperation agreement and in 2010, the [Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") hosted Greece's [Hellenic Air Force](/wiki/Hellenic_Air_Force "Hellenic Air Force") in a joint exercise. The joint Cyprus-Israel oil and gas explorations centered on the [Leviathan gas field](/wiki/Leviathan_gas_field "Leviathan gas field") are an important factor for Greece, given its [strong links](/wiki/Cyprus%E2%80%93Greece_relations "Cyprus–Greece relations") with Cyprus.[[463]](#cite_note-472) Cooperation in the world's longest [submarine power cable](/wiki/Submarine_power_cable "Submarine power cable"), the [EuroAsia Interconnector](/wiki/EuroAsia_Interconnector "EuroAsia Interconnector"), has strengthened [Cyprus–Israel relations](/wiki/Cyprus%E2%80%93Israel_relations "Cyprus–Israel relations").[[464]](#cite_note-473)

Azerbaijan is one of the few majority Muslim countries to develop strategic and economic [relations with Israel](/wiki/Azerbaijan%E2%80%93Israel_relations "Azerbaijan–Israel relations").[[465]](#cite_note-adalah-474) Kazakhstan also has an economic and strategic partnership with Israel.[[466]](#cite_note-475) India established full [diplomatic ties](/wiki/India%E2%80%93Israel_relations "India–Israel relations") with Israel in 1992 and has fostered a strong military, technological and cultural partnership with the country since then.[[467]](#cite_note-476) India is the largest customer of the [Israeli military equipment](/wiki/Israeli_military_equipment "Israeli military equipment"), and Israel is the second-largest military partner of India after Russia.[[468]](#cite_note-477) [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia%E2%80%93Israel_relations "Ethiopia–Israel relations") is Israel's main ally in Africa due to common political, religious and security interests.[[469]](#cite_note-478)

As of 2025, Israel is the only UN member state to recognize the [Republic of Somaliland](/wiki/Republic_of_Somaliland "Republic of Somaliland").[[470]](#cite_note-Somaliland-479)[[471]](#cite_note-DaV6b-480)[[472]](#cite_note-T3L29-481)

#### Foreign aid

Israel has a history of providing emergency foreign aid and humanitarian response to disasters across the world.[[473]](#cite_note-482) In 1955 Israel began its [foreign aid programme](/wiki/Israeli_foreign_aid "Israeli foreign aid") in Burma and then shifted to Africa.[[474]](#cite_note-Geldenhuys-483) Israel's humanitarian efforts officially began in 1957 with the establishment of [Mashav](/wiki/Mashav "Mashav"), the Israel's Agency for International Development Cooperation.[[475]](#cite_note-484) In this early period, whilst Israel's aid represented only a small percentage of total aid to Africa, its programme was effective in creating goodwill; however, following the 1967 war relations soured.[[476]](#cite_note-Ismael-485) Israel's foreign aid programme subsequently shifted its focus to Latin America.[[474]](#cite_note-Geldenhuys-483)

Since the late 1970s Israel's foreign aid has gradually decreased, although in recent years Israel has tried to reestablish aid to Africa.[[477]](#cite_note-486) There are additional Israeli humanitarian and emergency response groups that work with the government, including [IsraAid](/wiki/IsraAid "IsraAid"), a joint programme run by Israeli organisations and North American Jewish groups,[[478]](#cite_note-487) [ZAKA](/wiki/ZAKA "ZAKA"),[[479]](#cite_note-488) The Fast Israeli Rescue and Search Team,[[480]](#cite_note-489) Israeli Flying Aid,[[481]](#cite_note-490) [Save a Child's Heart](/wiki/Save_a_Child%27s_Heart "Save a Child's Heart")[[482]](#cite_note-491) and [Latet](/wiki/Latet "Latet").[[483]](#cite_note-492) Between 1985 and 2015, Israel sent 24 delegations of their search and rescue unit the [Home Front Command](/wiki/Home_Front_Command "Home Front Command") to 22 countries.[[484]](#cite_note-493) Currently Israeli foreign aid [ranks](/wiki/List_of_development_aid_country_donors "List of development aid country donors") low among [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") nations, spending less than 0.1% of its [GNI](/wiki/Gross_national_income "Gross national income") on development assistance.[[485]](#cite_note-494) The country ranked 38th in the 2018 [World Giving Index](/wiki/World_Giving_Index "World Giving Index").[[486]](#cite_note-495)

### Military

Further information: [List of wars involving Israel](/wiki/List_of_wars_involving_Israel "List of wars involving Israel"), [List of the Israel Defense Forces operations](/wiki/List_of_the_Israel_Defense_Forces_operations "List of the Israel Defense Forces operations"), and [Israel and weapons of mass destruction](/wiki/Israel_and_weapons_of_mass_destruction "Israel and weapons of mass destruction")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/The_Southern_Lions._Squadron_116._I.jpg/250px-The_Southern_Lions._Squadron_116._I.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Southern_Lions._Squadron_116._I.jpg)

[F-35](/wiki/F-35 "F-35") fighter jets of the [Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force")

The [Israel Defence Forces](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") (IDF) is the sole military wing of the [Israeli security forces](/wiki/Israeli_security_forces "Israeli security forces") and is headed by its [Chief of the General Staff](/wiki/Chief_of_the_General_Staff_(Israel) "Chief of the General Staff (Israel)"), the *Ramatkal*, subordinate to the Cabinet. The IDF consists of the [army](/wiki/GOC_Army_Headquarters "GOC Army Headquarters"), [air force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") and [navy](/wiki/Israeli_Navy "Israeli Navy"). It was founded during the 1948 Arab–Israeli War by consolidating paramilitary organisations, chiefly the Haganah.[[487]](#cite_note-496) The IDF also draws upon the resources of the [Military Intelligence Directorate](/wiki/Military_Intelligence_Directorate_(Israel) "Military Intelligence Directorate (Israel)") (*Aman*).[[488]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenderson200397-497)

Most [Israelis are conscripted](/wiki/Conscription_in_Israel "Conscription in Israel") at age 18. Men serve two years and eight months, and [women serve](/wiki/Women_in_the_Israel_Defense_Forces "Women in the Israel Defense Forces") two years.[[489]](#cite_note-498) Following mandatory service, Israeli men join the reserve forces and usually do up to several weeks of [reserve duty](/wiki/Reserve_duty_(Israel) "Reserve duty (Israel)") every year until their forties. Most women are exempt from reserve duty. [Arab citizens of Israel](/wiki/Arab_citizens_of_Israel "Arab citizens of Israel") (except the [Druze](/wiki/Druze_in_Israel "Druze in Israel")) and those engaged in full-time religious studies [are exempt](/wiki/Exemption_from_military_service_in_Israel "Exemption from military service in Israel"), although the [exemption of yeshiva students](/wiki/Tal_committee "Tal committee") has been a source of contention.[[490]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStendel1997191–192-499)[[491]](#cite_note-500) An alternative for those who receive exemptions on various grounds is *[Sherut Leumi](/wiki/Sherut_Leumi "Sherut Leumi")*, or national service, which involves a programme of service in social welfare frameworks.[[492]](#cite_note-501) A small minority of Israeli Arabs also volunteer in the army.[[493]](#cite_note-502) As a result of its conscription programme, the IDF maintains approximately 176,500 active troops and 465,000 reservists, giving Israel one of the world's highest [percentage of citizens with military training](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_military_and_paramilitary_personnel "List of countries by number of military and paramilitary personnel").[[494]](#cite_note-IISS_military-503)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/IDF_Air_Defense_fighters_during_Operation_Guardian_of_the_Walls%2C_May_2021._I.jpg/250px-IDF_Air_Defense_fighters_during_Operation_Guardian_of_the_Walls%2C_May_2021._I.jpg)](/wiki/File:IDF_Air_Defense_fighters_during_Operation_Guardian_of_the_Walls,_May_2021._I.jpg)

[Iron Dome](/wiki/Iron_Dome "Iron Dome") is the world's first operational anti-[artillery rocket](/wiki/Rocket_artillery "Rocket artillery") defence system.

The military relies heavily on high-tech [weapons](/wiki/Military_equipment_of_Israel "Military equipment of Israel") systems [designed and manufactured in Israel](/wiki/Defense_industry_of_Israel "Defense industry of Israel") as well as some foreign imports. The [Arrow](/wiki/Arrow_(Israeli_missile) "Arrow (Israeli missile)") missile is one of the world's few operational [anti-ballistic missile](/wiki/Anti-ballistic_missile "Anti-ballistic missile") systems.[[495]](#cite_note-504) The [Python](/wiki/Python_(missile) "Python (missile)") air-to-air missile series is often considered one of the most crucial weapons in its military history.[[496]](#cite_note-505) Israel's [Spike](/wiki/Spike_(missile) "Spike (missile)") missile is one of the most widely exported [anti-tank guided missiles](/wiki/Anti-tank_guided_missile "Anti-tank guided missile") in the world.[[497]](#cite_note-506) Israel's [Iron Dome](/wiki/Iron_Dome "Iron Dome") anti-missile air defence system gained worldwide acclaim after intercepting hundreds of [rockets fired by Palestinian militants](/wiki/Palestinian_rocket_attacks_on_Israel "Palestinian rocket attacks on Israel") from the Gaza Strip.[[498]](#cite_note-Johnson-507)[[499]](#cite_note-Tory-508) Since the [Yom Kippur War](/wiki/Yom_Kippur_War "Yom Kippur War"), Israel has developed a network of [reconnaissance satellites](/wiki/Reconnaissance_satellites "Reconnaissance satellites").[[500]](#cite_note-509) The *[Ofeq](/wiki/Ofeq "Ofeq")* programme has made Israel [one of seven countries](/wiki/Timeline_of_first_orbital_launches_by_country "Timeline of first orbital launches by country") capable of launching such satellites.[[501]](#cite_note-510)

Israel is widely believed to [possess nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_and_Israel "Nuclear weapons and Israel")[[502]](#cite_note-511) and per a 1993 report, chemical and biological [weapons of mass destruction](/wiki/Israel_and_weapons_of_mass_destruction "Israel and weapons of mass destruction").[[503]](#cite_note-512)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] Israel has not signed the [Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Non-Proliferation_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons")[[504]](#cite_note-513) and maintains a [policy of deliberate ambiguity](/wiki/Policy_of_deliberate_ambiguity "Policy of deliberate ambiguity") towards its nuclear capabilities.[[505]](#cite_note-514) The Israeli Navy's [Dolphin submarines](/wiki/Dolphin-class_submarine "Dolphin-class submarine") are believed to be armed with nuclear missiles offering [second-strike](/wiki/Second_strike "Second strike") capability.[[506]](#cite_note-515) Since the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War") in 1991, all homes in Israel are required to have a reinforced security room, [Merkhav Mugan](/wiki/Merkhav_Mugan "Merkhav Mugan"), impermeable to chemical and biological substances.[[507]](#cite_note-516)

Since Israel's establishment, military expenditure constituted a significant portion of the country's [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product"), with peak of 30.3% of GDP in 1975.[[508]](#cite_note-517) In 2021, Israel ranked 15th in the world [by total military expenditure](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures "List of countries by military expenditures"), with $24.3 billion, and 6th by defence spending as a percentage of GDP, with 5.2%.[[509]](#cite_note-518) Since 1974, the United States has been a particularly notable contributor of [military aid](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93United_States_military_relations#Military_aid_and_procurement "Israel–United States military relations").[[510]](#cite_note-519) Under a [memorandum of understanding](/wiki/Memorandum_of_understanding "Memorandum of understanding") signed in 2016, the U.S. is expected to provide the country with $3.8 billion per year, or around 20% of Israel's defence budget, from 2018 to 2028.[[511]](#cite_note-520) Israel ranked 8th globally for [arms exports](/wiki/Arms_industry "Arms industry") in 2020–2024.[[512]](#cite_note-521) The majority of Israel's arms exports are unreported for security reasons.[[513]](#cite_note-522) Israel is consistently rated low in the [Global Peace Index](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index"), ranking 134th out of 163 nations in 2022.[[514]](#cite_note-GPI-523)

### Legal system

Main articles: [Judiciary of Israel](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Israel "Judiciary of Israel") and [Israeli law](/wiki/Israeli_law "Israeli law")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Israel_Supreme_Court.jpg/250px-Israel_Supreme_Court.jpg)](/wiki/File:Israel_Supreme_Court.jpg)

[Supreme Court of Israel](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Israel "Supreme Court of Israel"), Givat Ram, Jerusalem

Israel has a [three-tier court system](/wiki/Israeli_judicial_system "Israeli judicial system"). At the lowest level are [magistrate](/wiki/Magistrate "Magistrate") courts, situated in most cities across the country. Above them are [district courts](/wiki/District_court "District court"), serving as both [appellate](/wiki/Appeal "Appeal") courts and [courts of first instance](/wiki/Trial_court "Trial court"); they are situated in five of Israel's six [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Israel "Districts of Israel"). The third and highest tier is the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Israel "Supreme Court of Israel"), located in Jerusalem; it serves a dual role as the highest court of appeals and the [High Court of Justice](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice_(Israel) "High Court of Justice (Israel)"). In the latter role, the Supreme Court rules as a court of first instance, allowing both citizens and non-citizens to petition against the decisions of state authorities.[[515]](#cite_note-524)

The legal system combines three legal traditions: [English common law](/wiki/English_law "English law"), [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)"), and [Jewish law](/wiki/Halakha "Halakha").[[317]](#cite_note-cia-326) It is based on the principle of *[stare decisis](/wiki/Stare_decisis "Stare decisis")* (precedent) and is an [adversarial system](/wiki/Adversarial_system "Adversarial system"). Court cases are decided by professional judges.[[516]](#cite_note-judiciary-525)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] [Marriage](/wiki/Marriage_in_Israel "Marriage in Israel") and divorce are under the jurisdiction of the religious courts: [Jewish](/wiki/Beth_din "Beth din"), [Muslim](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia"), Druze, and Christian. The election of judges is carried out by a [selection committee](/wiki/Judicial_Selection_Committee_(Israel) "Judicial Selection Committee (Israel)") chaired by the [justice minister](/wiki/Ministry_of_Justice_(Israel) "Ministry of Justice (Israel)") (currently [Yariv Levin](/wiki/Yariv_Levin "Yariv Levin")).[[517]](#cite_note-526) Israel's [Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty](/wiki/Basic_Law:_Human_Dignity_and_Liberty "Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty") seeks to defend [human rights and liberties in Israel](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Israel "Human rights in Israel"). The [United Nations Human Rights Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Human_Rights_Council "United Nations Human Rights Council") and Israeli human rights organisation [Adalah](/wiki/Adalah_(legal_center) "Adalah (legal center)") have highlighted that this law does not in fact contain a general provision for equality and non-discrimination.[[465]](#cite_note-adalah-474)[[518]](#cite_note-527) As a result of "[Enclave law](/wiki/Enclave_law "Enclave law")", large portions of Israeli civil law are applied to Israeli settlements and Israeli residents in the occupied territories.[[519]](#cite_note-Ben-NaftaliSfard2018-528) Israeli law is seldom enforced in the occupied territories, with 96% of police investigations into settler violence in the West Bank from 2020 to 2025 ending without an indictment. No one has been charged in the killings of at least 1,100 Palestinian civilians by Israeli soldiers and settlers since 2020.[[520]](#cite_note-529)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Israel](/wiki/Economy_of_Israel "Economy of Israel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Ayalon_Highway%2C_Tel_Aviv_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Ayalon_Highway%2C_Tel_Aviv_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ayalon_Highway,_Tel_Aviv_(cropped).jpg)

The [Diamond Exchange District](/wiki/Diamond_Exchange_District "Diamond Exchange District") in [Ramat Gan](/wiki/Ramat_Gan "Ramat Gan")

Israel is considered the most advanced country in [West Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia") and the Middle East in economic and industrial development.[[521]](#cite_note-Chua_2003_219–220-530)[[522]](#cite_note-531) As of October 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Israel&action=edit), the IMF estimated its GDP at 521.7 billion dollars and GDP per capita at 53.2 thousand ([ranking 13th worldwide](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (nominal) per capita")).[[523]](#cite_note-532) It is the third richest country in Asia [by nominal per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (nominal) per capita") income[[524]](#cite_note-533) and has the highest average [wealth per adult](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_wealth_per_adult "List of countries by wealth per adult") in the Middle East.[[525]](#cite_note-CS_2019-1-534) *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")* ranked Israel as the fourth most successful economy among the developed countries for 2022.[[526]](#cite_note-535) It has the [most billionaires](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_billionaires "List of countries by number of billionaires") in the Middle East and the 18th most in the world.[[527]](#cite_note-536) In recent years, Israel had one of the highest growth rates in the developed world.[[528]](#cite_note-537) In 2010, it joined the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD").[[529]](#cite_note-OECD-538)[[530]](#cite_note-539) The country is ranked 35th on the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank")'s [*Ease of Doing Business* index](/wiki/Ease_of_doing_business_index "Ease of doing business index").[[531]](#cite_note-540) Economic data covers the economic territory of Israel, including the Golan Heights, East Jerusalem and Israeli settlements in the West Bank.[[366]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOECD2011-375)

Despite limited natural resources, intensive development of the [agricultural](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Israel "Agriculture in Israel") and industrial sectors over the past decades has made Israel largely self-sufficient in food production, apart from grains and beef. Imports, totalling $96.5 billion in 2020, include raw materials, military equipment, investment goods, rough diamonds, fuels, grain, and consumer goods.[[317]](#cite_note-cia-326) Leading exports include machinery, equipment, software, [cut diamonds](/wiki/Diamond_industry_in_Israel "Diamond industry in Israel"), agricultural products, chemicals, textiles, and apparel; in 2020, exports reached $114 billion.[[317]](#cite_note-cia-326) The [Bank of Israel](/wiki/Bank_of_Israel "Bank of Israel") holds $201 billion of foreign-exchange reserves, the 17th highest in the world.[[317]](#cite_note-cia-326) Since the 1970s, Israel has received [military aid](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93United_States_military_relations "Israel–United States military relations") from the United States, as well as loan guarantees, which account for roughly half of Israel's external debt. Israel has [one of the lowest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_external_debt "List of countries by external debt") external debts in the developed world, and is a lender in terms of net external debt ([assets vs. liabilities abroad](/wiki/Net_international_investment_position "Net international investment position")), which in 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Israel&action=edit) stood at a surplus of $69 billion.[[532]](#cite_note-541)

Israel has the second-largest number of startup companies after the United States[[533]](#cite_note-542) and the third-largest number of [NASDAQ-listed companies](/wiki/List_of_Israeli_companies_quoted_on_the_Nasdaq "List of Israeli companies quoted on the Nasdaq").[[534]](#cite_note-543) It is the world leader for number of start-ups per capita[[535]](#cite_note-544) and has been dubbed the "[Start-Up Nation](/wiki/Start-up_Nation "Start-up Nation")".[[536]](#cite_note-545)[[537]](#cite_note-546)[[538]](#cite_note-547)[[539]](#cite_note-548) [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel")[[540]](#cite_note-549) and [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft")[[541]](#cite_note-550) built their first overseas [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") facilities in Israel, and other high-tech multinational corporations have opened [research and development centres in the country](/wiki/List_of_multinational_companies_with_research_and_development_centres_in_Israel "List of multinational companies with research and development centres in Israel").

The days which are allocated to working times are Sunday through Thursday (for a five-day workweek), or Friday (for a six-day workweek). In observance of [Shabbat](/wiki/Shabbat "Shabbat"), in places where Friday is a work day and the majority of population is Jewish, Friday is a "short day". Several proposals have been raised to adjust the work week with the majority of the world.[[542]](#cite_note-551)

### Science and technology

Main articles: [Science and technology in Israel](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Israel "Science and technology in Israel") and [List of Israeli inventions and discoveries](/wiki/List_of_Israeli_inventions_and_discoveries "List of Israeli inventions and discoveries")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Matam_hi-tech_park_%28Haifa%29.jpg/250px-Matam_hi-tech_park_%28Haifa%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matam_hi-tech_park_(Haifa).jpg)

[Matam](/wiki/Matam,_Haifa "Matam, Haifa") high-tech park in Haifa

Israel's development of cutting-edge technologies in software, communications and the life sciences have [evoked comparisons](/wiki/Silicon_Wadi "Silicon Wadi") with [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley").[[543]](#cite_note-552)[[544]](#cite_note-553) Israel is first in the world in [expenditure on research and development](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_research_and_development_spending "List of countries by research and development spending") as a percentage of GDP.[[545]](#cite_note-OECD_R&D-554) It is ranked 14th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025,[[546]](#cite_note-555)[[547]](#cite_note-556) and 5th in the 2019 [Bloomberg Innovation Index](/wiki/Bloomberg_Innovation_Index "Bloomberg Innovation Index").[[548]](#cite_note-Bloomberg_innovation-557) Israel has 140 scientists, technicians, and engineers per 10,000 employees, the highest number in the world[[549]](#cite_note-558)[[550]](#cite_note-559) and has produced six [Nobel Prize-winning](/wiki/List_of_Israeli_Nobel_laureates "List of Israeli Nobel laureates") scientists, mostly in chemistry, since 2004[[551]](#cite_note-nobel-560) and has been frequently ranked as one of the countries with the highest ratios of [scientific papers](/wiki/Scientific_papers "Scientific papers") per capita.[[552]](#cite_note-561)[[553]](#cite_note-562)[[554]](#cite_note-563) [Israeli universities](/wiki/List_of_Israeli_universities_and_colleges "List of Israeli universities and colleges") are ranked among the top 50 world universities in computer science ([Technion](/wiki/Technion "Technion") and [Tel Aviv University](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_University "Tel Aviv University")), mathematics ([Hebrew University of Jerusalem](/wiki/Hebrew_University_of_Jerusalem "Hebrew University of Jerusalem")) and chemistry ([Weizmann Institute of Science](/wiki/Weizmann_Institute_of_Science "Weizmann Institute of Science")).[[555]](#cite_note-ARWU-564)

In 2012, Israel was ranked ninth in the world by the Futron's [Space Competitiveness Index](/wiki/Space_Competitiveness_Index "Space Competitiveness Index").[[556]](#cite_note-565) The [Israel Space Agency](/wiki/Israel_Space_Agency "Israel Space Agency") coordinates all space research programmes with scientific and commercial goals, and have designed and built at least 13 commercial, research and spy satellites.[[557]](#cite_note-566) Some satellites are ranked among the world's most advanced space systems.[[558]](#cite_note-567) [Shavit](/wiki/Shavit_2 "Shavit 2") is a space [launch vehicle](/wiki/Launch_vehicle "Launch vehicle") produced by Israel to launch small satellites into [low Earth orbit](/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit "Low Earth orbit").[[559]](#cite_note-568) It was first launched in 1988, making Israel the [eighth nation](/wiki/Timeline_of_first_orbital_launches_by_country "Timeline of first orbital launches by country") to have a space launch capability. In 2003, [Ilan Ramon](/wiki/Ilan_Ramon "Ilan Ramon") became Israel's first astronaut, serving on the [fatal mission](/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Columbia_disaster "Space Shuttle Columbia disaster") of [Space Shuttle *Columbia*](/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Columbia "Space Shuttle Columbia").[[560]](#cite_note-569)

The [ongoing water shortage](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Israel "Water supply and sanitation in Israel") has spurred innovation in water conservation techniques, and a substantial [agricultural modernisation](/wiki/Agricultural_research_in_Israel "Agricultural research in Israel"), [drip irrigation](/wiki/Drip_irrigation "Drip irrigation"), was invented in Israel. Israel is also at the technological forefront of desalination and water recycling. The Sorek desalination plant is the largest seawater [reverse osmosis](/wiki/Reverse_osmosis "Reverse osmosis") desalination facility in the world.[[561]](#cite_note-570) By 2014, desalination programmes provided roughly 35% of the drinking water, and it is expected to supply 70% by 2050.[[562]](#cite_note-571) As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Israel&action=edit), over 50 per cent of the water for households, agriculture and industry is artificially produced.[[563]](#cite_note-Kershner-572) In 2011, Israel's water technology industry was worth around $2 billion per year with annual exports of products and services in the tens of millions of dollars. As a result of innovations in reverse osmosis technology, Israel is set to become a net [exporter of water](/wiki/Water_export "Water export").[[564]](#cite_note-573)

[![A horizontal parabolic dish, with a triangular structure on its top.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Solar_dish_at_Ben-Gurion_National_Solar_Energy_Center_in_Israel.jpg/250px-Solar_dish_at_Ben-Gurion_National_Solar_Energy_Center_in_Israel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Solar_dish_at_Ben-Gurion_National_Solar_Energy_Center_in_Israel.jpg)

The world's largest [solar parabolic dish](/wiki/Parabolic_reflector "Parabolic reflector") at the [Ben-Gurion National Solar Energy Center](/wiki/Ben-Gurion_National_Solar_Energy_Center "Ben-Gurion National Solar Energy Center")[[565]](#cite_note-Register-574)

Israel has embraced [solar energy](/wiki/Solar_power_in_Israel "Solar power in Israel"); its engineers are on the cutting edge of solar energy technology,[[566]](#cite_note-NPR-575) and its solar companies work on projects around the world.[[567]](#cite_note-CBC-576)[[568]](#cite_note-BW-577) Over 90% of homes use solar energy for hot water, the highest per capita.[[340]](#cite_note-Solar_energy-349)[[569]](#cite_note-Environment_California_SWH-578) According to government figures, the country saves 8% of its electricity consumption per year because of its solar energy use in heating.[[570]](#cite_note-579) The high annual incident solar irradiance at its geographic latitude creates ideal conditions for what is an internationally renowned solar research and development industry in the Negev.[[566]](#cite_note-NPR-575)[[567]](#cite_note-CBC-576)[[568]](#cite_note-BW-577) Israel had a modern electric car infrastructure involving a countrywide network of charging stations;[[571]](#cite_note-580)[[572]](#cite_note-581)[[573]](#cite_note-582) however, its electric car company [Better Place](/wiki/Better_Place_(company) "Better Place (company)") shut down in 2013.[[574]](#cite_note-583)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Israel](/wiki/Energy_in_Israel "Energy in Israel")

Israel [began producing natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas_in_Israel "Natural gas in Israel") from its own offshore gas fields in 2004. In 2009 [Tamar gas field](/wiki/Tamar_gas_field "Tamar gas field") was discovered near the coast, and [Leviathan gas field](/wiki/Leviathan_gas_field "Leviathan gas field") was discovered in 2010.[[575]](#cite_note-584) The natural gas reserves in these two fields could make Israel energy-secure for more than 50 years. Commercial production of natural gas from the Tamar field began in 2013, with over 7.5 billion cubic metres (bcm) produced annually.[[576]](#cite_note-585) Israel had 199 billion bcm of proven reserves of natural gas as of 2016.[[577]](#cite_note-586) The Leviathan gas field started production in 2019.[[578]](#cite_note-587)

[Ketura Sun](/wiki/Ketura_Sun "Ketura Sun") is Israel's first commercial solar field. Built in 2011 by the [Arava Power Company](/wiki/Arava_Power_Company "Arava Power Company"), the field will produce about 9 gigawatt-hours (32 TJ) of electricity per year,[[579]](#cite_note-588) sparing the production of some 125,000 tonnes (123,000 long tons; 138,000 short tons) of carbon dioxide over 20 years.[[580]](#cite_note-589)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Israel](/wiki/Transport_in_Israel "Transport in Israel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/4X-ECC_LLBG_09-05-2014b.jpg/250px-4X-ECC_LLBG_09-05-2014b.jpg)](/wiki/File:4X-ECC_LLBG_09-05-2014b.jpg)

[Ben Gurion International Airport](/wiki/Ben_Gurion_International_Airport "Ben Gurion International Airport"), the main international airport of Israel

As of 2016, Israel has 19,224 kilometres (11,945 mi) of paved [roads](/wiki/Roads_in_Israel "Roads in Israel")[[581]](#cite_note-590) and 3 million motor vehicles.[[582]](#cite_note-vehicles-591) The [number of motor vehicles per 1,000 persons](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_vehicles_per_capita "List of countries by vehicles per capita") is 365, relatively low among developed countries.[[582]](#cite_note-vehicles-591) The country aims to have 30% of vehicles on its roads powered by electricity by 2030.[[583]](#cite_note-592)

Israel has 5,715 buses on scheduled routes,[[584]](#cite_note-593) operated by several carriers, the largest and oldest of which is [Egged](/wiki/Egged_(company) "Egged (company)"), serving most of the country.[[585]](#cite_note-594) [Railways](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Israel "Rail transport in Israel") stretch across 1,277 kilometres (793 mi) and are operated by government-owned [Israel Railways](/wiki/Israel_Railways "Israel Railways").[[586]](#cite_note-cbs_rails-595) Following major investments beginning in the early to mid-1990s, the number of train passengers per year has grown from 2.5 million in 1990, to 53 million in 2015; railways transport 7.5 million tonnes (8.3 million short tons; 7.4 million long tons) of cargo per year.[[586]](#cite_note-cbs_rails-595)

Israel is served by three international [airports](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Israel "List of airports in Israel"): [Ben Gurion Airport](/wiki/Ben_Gurion_Airport "Ben Gurion Airport"), the country's main hub for international air travel; [Ramon Airport](/wiki/Ramon_Airport "Ramon Airport"); and [Haifa Airport](/wiki/Haifa_Airport "Haifa Airport"). Ben Gurion handled over 21.1 million passengers in 2023.[[587]](#cite_note-596) There are three main ports: the [Port of Haifa](/wiki/Port_of_Haifa "Port of Haifa"), the oldest and largest; [Ashdod Port](/wiki/Port_of_Ashdod "Port of Ashdod"); and the [Port of Eilat](/wiki/Port_of_Eilat "Port of Eilat") on the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea").

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Israel](/wiki/Tourism_in_Israel "Tourism in Israel")

See also: [List of archaeological sites in Israel and Palestine](/wiki/List_of_archaeological_sites_in_Israel_and_Palestine "List of archaeological sites in Israel and Palestine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Ein_Bokek_-_Dead_Sea2.jpg/250px-Ein_Bokek_-_Dead_Sea2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ein_Bokek_-_Dead_Sea2.jpg)

[Ein Bokek](/wiki/Ein_Bokek "Ein Bokek") resort on the shore of the [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea")

Tourism, especially [religious tourism](/wiki/Religious_tourism "Religious tourism"), is an important industry, with [beaches](/wiki/List_of_beaches_in_Israel "List of beaches in Israel"), [archaeological](/wiki/Archaeology_of_Israel "Archaeology of Israel"), other [historical](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Israel "List of World Heritage Sites in Israel") and [biblical](/wiki/List_of_biblical_places "List of biblical places") sites, and unique geography also drawing tourists. In 2017, a record 3.6 million tourists visited Israel, yielding a 25 per cent growth since 2016 and contributed NIS 20 billion to the economy.[[588]](#cite_note-597)[[589]](#cite_note-598)

### Real estate

Main article: [Housing in Israel](/wiki/Housing_in_Israel "Housing in Israel")

Housing prices are listed in the top third of all countries,[[590]](#cite_note-599) with an average of 150 salaries required to buy an apartment.[[591]](#cite_note-600) As of 2022, there are about 2.7 million properties in Israel, with an annual increase of over 50,000.[[592]](#cite_note-601) However, demand for housing exceeds supply, with a shortage of about 200,000 apartments as of 2021.[[593]](#cite_note-602) As a result, by 2021 housing prices rose by 5.6%.[[594]](#cite_note-603) In 2021, Israelis took a record of NIS 116.1 billion in mortgages, an increase of 50% from 2020.[[595]](#cite_note-604)

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of Israel](/wiki/Demographics_of_Israel "Demographics of Israel") and [Israelis](/wiki/Israelis "Israelis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Aliyah_1948-2015.png/330px-Aliyah_1948-2015.png)](/wiki/File:Aliyah_1948-2015.png)

[Immigration to Israel](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah") in the years 1948–2015. The two peaks were in 1949 and 1990.

Israel has the largest Jewish population in the world and is the only country where Jews are the majority,[[596]](#cite_note-605) and the only country in which Jews make up more than 2% of the total national population.[[597]](#cite_note-606) As of April 2025, the population was an estimated 10,094,000.[[598]](#cite_note-607) In 2025, the government recorded 72% of the population as [Jews](/wiki/Israeli_Jews "Israeli Jews"), 21% as [Arabs](/wiki/Arab_citizens_of_Israel "Arab citizens of Israel"), and 7% as "Others" (non-Arab Christians and people who have no religion listed).[[13]](#cite_note-population_stat2024-16) Over the last decade, large numbers of migrant workers from Romania, Thailand, China, Africa, and South America have settled in Israel. Exact figures are unknown, as many of them are living in the country illegally,[[599]](#cite_note-608) but estimates run from 166,000 to 203,000.[[600]](#cite_note-Adriana_Kemp-609) By June 2012, approximately 60,000 [African migrants](/wiki/Illegal_immigration_from_Africa_to_Israel "Illegal immigration from Africa to Israel") had entered Israel.[[601]](#cite_note-610)

About 93% of Israelis live in urban areas.[[602]](#cite_note-611) 90% of Palestinian Israelis reside in 139 densely populated towns and villages concentrated in the Galilee, [Triangle](/wiki/Triangle_(Israel) "Triangle (Israel)") and Negev regions, with the remaining 10% in [mixed cities](/wiki/Mixed_cities "Mixed cities") and neighbourhoods.[[603]](#cite_note-612) The OECD in 2016 estimated the average life expectancy at 82.5 years, the [6th-highest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_life_expectancy "List of countries by life expectancy").[[604]](#cite_note-OECD_life_expec-613) Israeli Arab life expectancy lags by 3 to 4 years[[605]](#cite_note-614)[[606]](#cite_note-615) and is higher than in most Arab and Muslim countries.[[607]](#cite_note-616)[[608]](#cite_note-617) The country has the highest [fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") in the OECD and the only one which is above the replacement figure of 2.1.[[609]](#cite_note-:5-618) Retention of Israel's population since 1948 is about even or greater, when compared to other countries with mass immigration.[[610]](#cite_note-619) Jewish emigration from Israel (called *[yerida](/wiki/Yerida "Yerida")*), primarily to the United States and Canada, is described by demographers as modest,[[611]](#cite_note-620) but is often cited by Israeli government ministries as a major threat to Israel's future.[[612]](#cite_note-621)[[613]](#cite_note-622)

Approximately 80% of [Israeli Jews](/wiki/Israeli_Jews "Israeli Jews") are [born in Israel](/wiki/Sabra_(person) "Sabra (person)"), 14% are immigrants from Europe and the Americas, and 6% are immigrants from Asia and Africa.[[614]](#cite_note-623) Jews from Europe and the former Soviet Union and their descendants born in Israel, including Ashkenazi Jews, constitute approximately 44% of Jewish Israelis. Jews from Arab and Muslim countries and their descendants, including both Mizrahi and Sephardi Jews,[[615]](#cite_note-624) form most of the rest of the Jewish population.[[616]](#cite_note-625) Jewish intermarriage rates run at over 35% and recent studies suggest that the percentage of Israelis descended from both Sephardi and Ashkenazi Jews increases by 0.5 per cent yearly, with over 25% of schoolchildren now originating from both.[[617]](#cite_note-626) Around 4% of Israelis (300,000), ethnically defined as "others", are Russian descendants of Jewish origin or family who are not Jewish according to rabbinical law, but were eligible for citizenship under the Law of Return.[[618]](#cite_note-627)[[619]](#cite_note-Israel_people-628)[[620]](#cite_note-629)

Israeli settlers beyond the Green Line number over 600,000 (≈10% of the Jewish Israeli population).[[621]](#cite_note-630) In 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Israel&action=edit), [399,300 Israelis lived](/wiki/Population_statistics_for_Israeli_settlements_in_the_West_Bank "Population statistics for Israeli settlements in the West Bank") in West Bank settlements,[[358]](#cite_note-districts_pop-367) including those that predated the establishment of the State of Israel and which were re-established after the Six-Day War. Additionally there were more than 200,000 Jews living in East Jerusalem[[359]](#cite_note-jerusalem_pop-368) and 22,000 in the Golan Heights.[[358]](#cite_note-districts_pop-367) Approximately 7,800 Israelis [lived in settlements](/wiki/Population_statistics_for_Israeli_Gaza_Strip_settlements "Population statistics for Israeli Gaza Strip settlements") in the Gaza Strip, known as [Gush Katif](/wiki/Gush_Katif "Gush Katif"), until they were evacuated by the government as part of its 2005 [disengagement plan](/wiki/Israel%27s_unilateral_disengagement_plan "Israel's unilateral disengagement plan").[[622]](#cite_note-631)

Israeli Arabs (including the Arab population of East Jerusalem and the Golan Heights) comprise 21.1% of the population or 1,995,000 people.[[623]](#cite_note-632) In a 2017 poll, 40% of Arab citizens of Israel identified as "Arab in Israel" or "Arab citizen of Israel", 15% identified as "Palestinian", 8.9% as "Palestinian in Israel" or "Palestinian citizen of Israel", and 8.7% as "Arab"; a poll found that 60% of Israeli Arabs have a positive view of the state.[[624]](#cite_note-633)[[625]](#cite_note-634)

### Major urban areas

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of cities in Israel](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Israel "List of cities in Israel").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Tel_Aviv_Panorama.jpg/960px-Tel_Aviv_Panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tel_Aviv_Panorama.jpg "View over the Tel Aviv Metropolitan Area")

View over the [Tel Aviv Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_Metropolitan_Area "Tel Aviv Metropolitan Area")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Israel_cities.svg/120px-Israel_cities.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Israel_cities.svg)

[Israel cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Israel "List of cities in Israel")

Israel has four major metropolitan areas: [Gush Dan](/wiki/Gush_Dan "Gush Dan") (Tel Aviv metropolitan area; population 3,854,000), [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem_metropolitan_area "Jerusalem metropolitan area") (population 1,253,900), [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa_metropolitan_area "Haifa metropolitan area") (924,400), and [Beersheba](/wiki/Beersheba_metropolitan_area "Beersheba metropolitan area") (377,100).[[626]](#cite_note-635) The largest municipality, in population and area, is Jerusalem with 1,050,153 residents in an area of 125 square kilometres (48 sq mi).[[627]](#cite_note-Israelpopulations-636) Statistics on Jerusalem include the population and area of East Jerusalem, the status of which is in international dispute.[[628]](#cite_note-637) Tel Aviv and Haifa rank as Israel's next most populous cities, with populations of 494,900 and 297,082, respectively.[[627]](#cite_note-Israelpopulations-636) The (mainly [Haredi](/wiki/Haredi "Haredi")) city of [Bnei Brak](/wiki/Bnei_Brak "Bnei Brak") is the most densely populated city in Israel and one of the [10 most densely populated cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_proper_by_population_density "List of cities proper by population density") in the world.[[629]](#cite_note-638)

Israel has 16 cities with populations over 100,000. As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Israel&action=edit) there are 77 localities granted ["municipalities" (or "city") status](/wiki/City_council_(Israel) "City council (Israel)") by the Ministry of the Interior,[[630]](#cite_note-639) [four of which are in the West Bank](/wiki/List_of_Israeli_settlements_with_city_status_in_the_West_Bank "List of Israeli settlements with city status in the West Bank").[[631]](#cite_note-640)
