### Language

Main article: [Languages of Israel](/wiki/Languages_of_Israel "Languages of Israel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/%D7%A9%D7%9C%D7%98_%D7%A8%D7%97%D7%95%D7%91_%D7%99%D7%94%D7%95%D7%93%D7%94_%283777232251%29.jpg/250px-%D7%A9%D7%9C%D7%98_%D7%A8%D7%97%D7%95%D7%91_%D7%99%D7%94%D7%95%D7%93%D7%94_%283777232251%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D7%A9%D7%9C%D7%98_%D7%A8%D7%97%D7%95%D7%91_%D7%99%D7%94%D7%95%D7%93%D7%94_(3777232251).jpg)

[Road sign](/wiki/Road_signs_in_Israel "Road signs in Israel") in [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language_in_Israel "Arabic language in Israel"), and [English](/wiki/English_language "English language")

The official language is [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"). Hebrew is the primary language of the state and is spoken daily by the majority of the population. Prior to 1948, [opposition](/wiki/Anti-Yiddish_sentiment "Anti-Yiddish sentiment") to [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish"), the historical language of the Ashkenazi Jews, was common among supporters of the Zionist movement, including the Yishuv, who sought to promote [Hebrew's revival](/wiki/Revival_of_the_Hebrew_language "Revival of the Hebrew language") as a unifying national language.[[633]](#cite_note-642) These sentiments were reflected in the early policies of the Israeli government, which largely banned [Yiddish theatre](/wiki/Yiddish_theatre "Yiddish theatre") and publications.[[634]](#cite_note-643) Until 2018, [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language_in_Israel "Arabic language in Israel") was also an official language;[[12]](#cite_note-lang1-14) in 2018 it was downgraded to having a "special status in the state".[[10]](#cite_note-lang2-12)[[11]](#cite_note-lang3-13) Arabic is spoken by the Arab minority, with Arabic and Hebrew taught in Arab schools.[[635]](#cite_note-arabic_lang-644) Arabic is studied in most Jewish schools and is often used on signage and in transport announcements.[[636]](#cite_note-645)

Due to mass immigration from the former Soviet Union and [Ethiopia](/wiki/Aliyah_from_Ethiopia "Aliyah from Ethiopia") (some 130,000 [Ethiopian Jews live in Israel](/wiki/Ethiopian_Jews_in_Israel "Ethiopian Jews in Israel")),[[637]](#cite_note-The_Ethiopian_Population_In_Israel-646)[[638]](#cite_note-647) [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language_in_Israel "Russian language in Israel") and [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic") are widely spoken.[[639]](#cite_note-648) Over one million Russian-speaking immigrants arrived in Israel between 1990 and 2004.[[640]](#cite_note-649) French is spoken by around 700,000 Israelis,[[641]](#cite_note-650) mostly originating [from France](/wiki/French_Jews_in_Israel "French Jews in Israel") and North Africa (see [Maghrebi Jews](/wiki/Maghrebi_Jews "Maghrebi Jews")). English was an official language during the Mandate period;[[642]](#cite_note-651) it lost this status after the establishment of Israel, but retains a role comparable to that of an official language.[[643]](#cite_note-652)[[644]](#cite_note-653) Many Israelis communicate reasonably well in English, as many television programmes are broadcast in English with subtitles and the language is taught in elementary school. Israeli universities offer courses in English.[[645]](#cite_note-654)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Israel](/wiki/Religion_in_Israel "Religion in Israel")

See also: [Abrahamic religions](/wiki/Abrahamic_religions "Abrahamic religions")

[![A large open area with people bounded by old stone walls. To the left is a mosque with large golden dome.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Westernwall2.jpg/250px-Westernwall2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Westernwall2.jpg)

The [Dome of the Rock](/wiki/Dome_of_the_Rock "Dome of the Rock") and the [Western Wall](/wiki/Western_Wall "Western Wall"), Jerusalem

The estimated religious affiliation as of 2022 was 73.5% Jewish, 18.1% [Muslim](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims"), 1.9% [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians"), 1.6% [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze"), and 4.9% other.[[646]](#cite_note-CIA2022-655) The [religious affiliation](/wiki/Jewish_religious_movements "Jewish religious movements") of [Israeli Jews](/wiki/Israeli_Jews "Israeli Jews") varies widely: a 2016 survey by [Pew Research](/wiki/Pew_Research "Pew Research") indicates that 49% self-identify as [Hiloni](/wiki/Hiloni "Hiloni") (secular), 29% as [Masorti](/wiki/Masortim "Masortim") (traditional), 13% as [Dati](/wiki/Dati "Dati") (religious) and 9% as [Haredi](/wiki/Haredi_Judaism "Haredi Judaism") (ultra-Orthodox).[[647]](#cite_note-656) Haredi Jews are expected to represent over 20% of the Jewish population by 2028.[[648]](#cite_note-657) [Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_Israel "Islam in Israel") constitute the largest religious minority, making up about 18.1% of the population. About 1.9% of the population is [Christian](/wiki/Christianity_in_Israel "Christianity in Israel"), and 1.6% is [Druze](/wiki/Druze_in_Israel "Druze in Israel").[[646]](#cite_note-CIA2022-655) The Christian population comprises primarily [Arab Christians](/wiki/Arab_Christians "Arab Christians") and [Aramean Christians](/wiki/Arameans_in_Israel "Arameans in Israel") but also includes post-Soviet immigrants, foreign labourers, [Armenian Christians](/wiki/Armenians_in_Israel_and_Palestine "Armenians in Israel and Palestine"), and followers of [Messianic Judaism](/wiki/Messianic_Judaism "Messianic Judaism"), considered by most Christians and Jews to be a form of Christianity.[[649]](#cite_note-658) Members of many other religious groups, including [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Hindus](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Israel "Hinduism in Israel"), maintain a presence in Israel, albeit in small numbers.[[650]](#cite_note-659) Out of over one million immigrants from the former Soviet Union, about 300,000 are considered not Jewish by the [Chief Rabbinate of Israel](/wiki/Chief_Rabbinate_of_Israel "Chief Rabbinate of Israel").[[651]](#cite_note-660)

Israel comprises a major part of the [Holy Land](/wiki/Holy_Land "Holy Land"), a region of significant importance to all [Abrahamic religions](/wiki/Abrahamic_religions "Abrahamic religions"). Jerusalem is of [special importance](/wiki/Religious_significance_of_Jerusalem "Religious significance of Jerusalem") to Jews, Muslims, and Christians, as it is the home of [sites](/wiki/List_of_places_in_Jerusalem "List of places in Jerusalem") that are pivotal to their religious beliefs, such as the [Old City](/wiki/Old_City_(Jerusalem) "Old City (Jerusalem)") that incorporates the [Western Wall](/wiki/Western_Wall "Western Wall") and the [Temple Mount](/wiki/Temple_Mount "Temple Mount") (Al-Aqsa Mosque compound) and the [Church of the Holy Sepulchre](/wiki/Church_of_the_Holy_Sepulchre "Church of the Holy Sepulchre").[[652]](#cite_note-661) Other locations of religious importance are [Nazareth](/wiki/Nazareth "Nazareth") (site of the [Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation") of [Mary](/wiki/Mary_(mother_of_Jesus) "Mary (mother of Jesus)")), [Tiberias](/wiki/Tiberias "Tiberias") and [Safed](/wiki/Safed "Safed") (two of the [Four Holy Cities](/wiki/Four_Holy_Cities "Four Holy Cities") in Judaism), the [White Mosque](/wiki/White_Mosque,_Ramla "White Mosque, Ramla") in [Ramla](/wiki/Ramla "Ramla") (shrine of the prophet [Saleh](/wiki/Salih "Salih")), and the [Church of Saint George and Mosque of Al-Khadr, Lod](/wiki/Church_of_Saint_George_and_Mosque_of_Al-Khadr,_Lod "Church of Saint George and Mosque of Al-Khadr, Lod") (tomb of [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George") or [Al Khidr](/wiki/Al_Khidr "Al Khidr")). A number of other religious landmarks are located in the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), including [Joseph's Tomb](/wiki/Joseph%27s_Tomb "Joseph's Tomb"), the [birthplace of Jesus](/wiki/Church_of_the_Nativity "Church of the Nativity"), [Rachel's Tomb](/wiki/Rachel%27s_Tomb "Rachel's Tomb"), and the [Cave of the Patriarchs](/wiki/Cave_of_the_Patriarchs "Cave of the Patriarchs"). The [administrative center](/wiki/Arc_(Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD) "Arc (Baháʼí)") of the [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith") and the [Shrine of the Báb](/wiki/Shrine_of_the_B%C3%A1b "Shrine of the Báb") are located at the [Baháʼí World Centre](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_World_Centre "Baháʼí World Centre") in [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa"); the leader of the faith is [buried](/wiki/Shrine_of_Bah%C3%A1%27u%27ll%C3%A1h "Shrine of Bahá'u'lláh") in [Acre](/wiki/Acre,_Israel "Acre, Israel").[[653]](#cite_note-662)[[654]](#cite_note-663)[[655]](#cite_note-664) The [Mahmood Mosque](/wiki/Mahmood_Mosque,_Haifa "Mahmood Mosque, Haifa") is affiliated with the reformist [Ahmadiyya](/wiki/Ahmadiyya_in_Israel "Ahmadiyya in Israel") movement. [Kababir](/wiki/Kababir "Kababir"), Haifa's mixed neighbourhood of Jews and Ahmadi Arabs, is one of a few of its kind in the country.[[656]](#cite_note-665)[[657]](#cite_note-666)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Israel](/wiki/Education_in_Israel "Education in Israel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Brain_research_labs-Bar_Ilan_university.jpg/250px-Brain_research_labs-Bar_Ilan_university.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brain_research_labs-Bar_Ilan_university.jpg)

[Multidisciplinary Brain Research Center](/wiki/Bar-Ilan_University#Leslie_and_Susan_Gonda_Multidisciplinary_Brain_Research_Center "Bar-Ilan University") at [Bar-Ilan University](/wiki/Bar-Ilan_University "Bar-Ilan University")

In 2015, Israel [ranked](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_tertiary_education_attainment "List of countries by tertiary education attainment") third among OECD members for the percentage of 25–64-year-olds that have attained tertiary education with 49% compared with the OECD average of 35%.[[658]](#cite_note-OECD_education-667) In 2012, the country ranked third in the number of academic degrees per capita (20 per cent of the population).[[659]](#cite_note-668)

Israel has a [school life expectancy](/wiki/School_life_expectancy "School life expectancy") of 16 years and a [literacy rate](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_literacy_rate "List of countries by literacy rate") of 97.8%.[[317]](#cite_note-cia-326) The State Education Law (1953) established five types of schools: state secular, state religious, ultra orthodox, communal settlement schools, and Arab schools. The public secular is the largest school group and is attended by the majority of Jewish and non-Arab pupils. Most Arabs send their children to schools where Arabic is the language of instruction.[[635]](#cite_note-arabic_lang-644) Education is compulsory for children between the ages of three and eighteen.[[660]](#cite_note-669) Schooling is divided into three tiers—primary school (grades 1–6), middle school (grades 7–9), and high school (grades 10–12)—culminating with *[Bagrut](/wiki/Bagrut "Bagrut")* matriculation exams. Proficiency in core subjects such as mathematics, the Hebrew language, Hebrew and general literature, the English language, history, Biblical scripture and civics is necessary to receive a Bagrut certificate.[[661]](#cite_note-moia-670)

The Jewish population maintains a relatively high level of educational attainment where just under half of all Israeli Jews (46%) hold post-secondary degrees.[[662]](#cite_note-671)[[663]](#cite_note-672) Israeli Jews 25 and older have an average 11.6 years of schooling, making them one of the most highly educated of all major religious groups in the world.[[664]](#cite_note-673)[[665]](#cite_note-674) In Arab, Christian and Druze schools, the exam on Biblical studies is replaced by an exam on Muslim, Christian or Druze heritage, respectively.[[666]](#cite_note-675) In 2020, 68.7% of 12th graders earned a matriculation certificate.[[667]](#cite_note-676)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/MountScopusDec032022_03.jpg/250px-MountScopusDec032022_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:MountScopusDec032022_03.jpg)

[Mount Scopus](/wiki/Mount_Scopus "Mount Scopus") Campus of the [Hebrew University of Jerusalem](/wiki/Hebrew_University_of_Jerusalem "Hebrew University of Jerusalem")

Israel has a tradition of higher education where its university education has been largely responsible in spurring modern economic development.[[668]](#cite_note-677) Israel has [nine public universities subsidised by the state and 49 private colleges](/wiki/List_of_Israeli_universities_and_colleges "List of Israeli universities and colleges").[[661]](#cite_note-moia-670)[[669]](#cite_note-678)[[670]](#cite_note-679) The [Hebrew University of Jerusalem](/wiki/Hebrew_University_of_Jerusalem "Hebrew University of Jerusalem") houses the [National Library of Israel](/wiki/National_Library_of_Israel "National Library of Israel"), the world's largest repository of Judaica and Hebraica.[[671]](#cite_note-680) The [Technion](/wiki/Technion "Technion") and the Hebrew University consistently ranked among world's 100 top universities by [ARWU](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities") ranking.[[555]](#cite_note-ARWU-564) Other major universities include the [Weizmann Institute of Science](/wiki/Weizmann_Institute_of_Science "Weizmann Institute of Science"), [Tel Aviv University](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_University "Tel Aviv University"), [Ben-Gurion University of the Negev](/wiki/Ben-Gurion_University_of_the_Negev "Ben-Gurion University of the Negev"), [Bar-Ilan University](/wiki/Bar-Ilan_University "Bar-Ilan University"), the [University of Haifa](/wiki/University_of_Haifa "University of Haifa"), and the [Open University of Israel](/wiki/Open_University_of_Israel "Open University of Israel").

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Israel](/wiki/Culture_of_Israel "Culture of Israel")

Cultural diversity stems from its diverse population: Jews from various diaspora communities brought their cultural and religious traditions with them.[[672]](#cite_note-681) [Arab influences](/wiki/Arab_citizens_of_Israel#Culture "Arab citizens of Israel") are found in [architecture](/wiki/Architecture_of_Israel "Architecture of Israel"),[[673]](#cite_note-682) [music](/wiki/Music_of_Israel "Music of Israel"),[[674]](#cite_note-683) and [cuisine](/wiki/Israeli_cuisine "Israeli cuisine"),[[675]](#cite_note-684) among others.[[676]](#cite_note-MendelRanta2016p137-685) Israel is the only country where life revolves around the [Hebrew calendar](/wiki/Hebrew_calendar "Hebrew calendar"), which is aligned to the seasons in the Levant. [Public holidays](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Israel "Public holidays in Israel") are determined by the [Jewish holidays](/wiki/Jewish_holidays "Jewish holidays"), and except for [Yom HaAtzma'ut](/wiki/Yom_HaAtzma%27ut "Yom HaAtzma'ut") (Independence Day) there are no annual civil holidays. The official day of rest is Saturday, the [Jewish Sabbath](/wiki/Shabbat "Shabbat").[[677]](#cite_note-686)

### Literature

[Israeli literature](/wiki/Israeli_literature "Israeli literature") is primarily [poetry](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew_poetry "Modern Hebrew poetry") and prose written in Hebrew, as part of the [renaissance](/wiki/Revival_of_the_Hebrew_language "Revival of the Hebrew language") of Hebrew as a spoken language since the mid-19th century, although a small body of literature is published in other languages. By law, two copies of all works published in Israel must be deposited in the [National Library](/wiki/National_Library_of_Israel "National Library of Israel").[[678]](#cite_note-687) In 2016, 89 per cent of the 7,300 books transferred to the library were in Hebrew.[[679]](#cite_note-688)

In 1966, [Shmuel Yosef Agnon](/wiki/Shmuel_Yosef_Agnon "Shmuel Yosef Agnon") shared the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") with German Jewish author [Nelly Sachs](/wiki/Nelly_Sachs "Nelly Sachs").[[680]](#cite_note-689) Leading poets include [Yehuda Amichai](/wiki/Yehuda_Amichai "Yehuda Amichai"), [Nathan Alterman](/wiki/Nathan_Alterman "Nathan Alterman"), [Leah Goldberg](/wiki/Leah_Goldberg "Leah Goldberg"), and [Rachel Bluwstein](/wiki/Rachel_Bluwstein "Rachel Bluwstein").[[681]](#cite_note-690) Internationally famous contemporary novelists include [Amos Oz](/wiki/Amos_Oz "Amos Oz"), [Etgar Keret](/wiki/Etgar_Keret "Etgar Keret") and [David Grossman](/wiki/David_Grossman "David Grossman").[[682]](#cite_note-691)[[683]](#cite_note-692)

### Music and dance

Further information: [Music of Israel](/wiki/Music_of_Israel "Music of Israel") and [Dance in Israel](/wiki/Dance_in_Israel "Dance in Israel")

[![Several dozen musicians in formal dress, holding their instruments, behind a conductor](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Israel_Philharmonic_Orchestra.jpg/330px-Israel_Philharmonic_Orchestra.jpg)](/wiki/File:Israel_Philharmonic_Orchestra.jpg)

[Israel Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Israel_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Israel Philharmonic Orchestra") conducted by [Zubin Mehta](/wiki/Zubin_Mehta "Zubin Mehta")

[Israeli music](/wiki/Music_of_Israel "Music of Israel") includes [Mizrahi](/wiki/Mizrahi_music "Mizrahi music") and [Sephardic music](/wiki/Sephardic_music "Sephardic music"), [Hasidic](/wiki/Hasidic_Judaism "Hasidic Judaism") melodies, [Greek music](/wiki/Greek_music_in_Israel "Greek music in Israel"), [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), and [pop rock](/wiki/Pop_rock "Pop rock").[[684]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBroughtonEllinghamTrillo1999365–369-693)[[685]](#cite_note-694) The [Israel Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Israel_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Israel Philharmonic Orchestra")[[686]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBen-Sasson19851095-695)[[687]](#cite_note-696) has been in operation for over seventy years and performs more than two hundred concerts each year.[[688]](#cite_note-697) [Itzhak Perlman](/wiki/Itzhak_Perlman "Itzhak Perlman"), [Pinchas Zukerman](/wiki/Pinchas_Zukerman "Pinchas Zukerman") and [Ofra Haza](/wiki/Ofra_Haza "Ofra Haza") are among the internationally acclaimed musicians born in Israel. The country [has participated](/wiki/Israel_in_the_Eurovision_Song_Contest "Israel in the Eurovision Song Contest") in the [Eurovision Song Contest](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest "Eurovision Song Contest") nearly every year since 1973, winning it four times and hosting three times.[[689]](#cite_note-history-698) [Eilat](/wiki/Eilat "Eilat") has hosted its own international music festival, the [Red Sea Jazz Festival](/wiki/Red_Sea_Jazz_Festival "Red Sea Jazz Festival"), every summer since 1987.[[690]](#cite_note-699) The nation's canonical [folk songs](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") are known as "Songs of the Land of Israel".[[691]](#cite_note-700)

### Cinema and theatre

Main article: [Cinema of Israel](/wiki/Cinema_of_Israel "Cinema of Israel")

Ten Israeli films [have been final nominees](/wiki/List_of_Israeli_submissions_for_the_Academy_Award_for_Best_Foreign_Language_Film "List of Israeli submissions for the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film") for [Best Foreign Language Film](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Foreign_Language_Film "Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film") at the [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"). Palestinian Israeli filmmakers have made films dealing with the Arab-Israeli conflict and status of Palestinians within Israel, such as [Mohammad Bakri](/wiki/Mohammad_Bakri "Mohammad Bakri")'s 2002 film *[Jenin, Jenin](/wiki/Jenin,_Jenin "Jenin, Jenin")* and *[The Syrian Bride](/wiki/The_Syrian_Bride "The Syrian Bride")*.

Continuing the strong theatrical traditions of the [Yiddish theatre](/wiki/Yiddish_theatre "Yiddish theatre") in Eastern Europe, Israel maintains a vibrant theatre scene. Founded in 1918, [Habima Theatre](/wiki/Habima_Theatre "Habima Theatre") in Tel Aviv is Israel's oldest [repertory theatre](/wiki/Repertory_theatre "Repertory theatre") company and national theatre.[[692]](#cite_note-701) Other theatres include [Ohel](/wiki/Ohel_Theater "Ohel Theater"), [the Cameri](/wiki/Cameri_Theatre "Cameri Theatre") and [Gesher](/wiki/Gesher_Theater "Gesher Theater").[[693]](#cite_note-702)[[694]](#cite_note-703)

### Visual arts

Main article: [Visual arts in Israel](/wiki/Visual_arts_in_Israel "Visual arts in Israel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/%D7%A9%D7%90%D7%92%D7%AA_%D7%94%D7%90%D7%A8%D7%99%D7%94.jpg/250px-%D7%A9%D7%90%D7%92%D7%AA_%D7%94%D7%90%D7%A8%D7%99%D7%94.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D7%A9%D7%90%D7%92%D7%AA_%D7%94%D7%90%D7%A8%D7%99%D7%94.jpg)

*[The Roaring Lion](/wiki/The_Roaring_Lion_(sculpture) "The Roaring Lion (sculpture)")* sculpture by [Avraham Melnikov](/wiki/Avraham_Melnikov "Avraham Melnikov")

Israeli Jewish art has been particularly influenced by the [Kabbalah](/wiki/Kabbalah "Kabbalah"), the [Talmud](/wiki/Talmud "Talmud") and the [Zohar](/wiki/Zohar "Zohar"). Another art movement that held a prominent role in the 20th century was the [School of Paris](/wiki/School_of_Paris "School of Paris"). In the late 19th and early 20th century, the Yishuv's art was dominated by art trends emanating [Bezalel](/wiki/Bezalel_Academy_of_Arts_and_Design "Bezalel Academy of Arts and Design"). Beginning in the 1920s, the local art scene was heavily influenced by modern French art, first introduced by [Isaac Frenkel Frenel](/wiki/Yitzhak_Frenkel "Yitzhak Frenkel").[[695]](#cite_note-704)[[696]](#cite_note-705) Jewish masters of the [school of Paris](/wiki/School_of_Paris "School of Paris"), such as [Soutine](/wiki/Cha%C3%AFm_Soutine "Chaïm Soutine"), [Kikoine](/wiki/Michel_Kikoine "Michel Kikoine"), [Frenkel](/wiki/Yitzhak_Frenkel "Yitzhak Frenkel"), [Chagall](/wiki/Marc_Chagall "Marc Chagall") heavily influenced the development of Israeli art.[[697]](#cite_note-706)[[698]](#cite_note-:43-707) Israeli sculpture took inspiration from modern [European sculpture](/wiki/Art_of_Europe "Art of Europe") as well [Mesopotamian](/wiki/Art_of_Mesopotamia "Art of Mesopotamia"), [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyrian_sculpture "Assyrian sculpture") and local art.[[699]](#cite_note-:6-708)[[700]](#cite_note-709) [Avraham Melnikov](/wiki/Avraham_Melnikov "Avraham Melnikov")'s [The Roaring Lion](/wiki/The_Roaring_Lion_(sculpture) "The Roaring Lion (sculpture)"), David Polus' Alexander Zaid and [Ze'ev Ben Zvi](/wiki/Zeev_Ben-Zvi "Zeev Ben-Zvi")'s cubist sculpture exemplify some of the different streams in sculpture.[[699]](#cite_note-:6-708)[[701]](#cite_note-710)[[702]](#cite_note-711)

Common themes in art are the mystical cities of Safed and Jerusalem, the bohemian café culture of Tel Aviv, agricultural landscapes, biblical stories and war. Today Israeli art has delved into [optical art](/wiki/Optical_art "Optical art"), [AI art](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence_art "Artificial intelligence art"), [digital art](/wiki/Digital_art "Digital art") and the use of salt in sculpture.[[698]](#cite_note-:43-707)

### Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of Israel](/wiki/Architecture_of_Israel "Architecture of Israel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/%D7%9E%D7%95%D7%96%D7%99%D7%90%D7%95%D7%9F_%D7%94%D7%91%D7%90%D7%95%D7%94%D7%90%D7%95%D7%A11.jpg/250px-%D7%9E%D7%95%D7%96%D7%99%D7%90%D7%95%D7%9F_%D7%94%D7%91%D7%90%D7%95%D7%94%D7%90%D7%95%D7%A11.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D7%9E%D7%95%D7%96%D7%99%D7%90%D7%95%D7%9F_%D7%94%D7%91%D7%90%D7%95%D7%94%D7%90%D7%95%D7%A11.jpg)

[Bauhaus Museum Tel Aviv](/wiki/Bauhaus_Foundation_Tel_Aviv "Bauhaus Foundation Tel Aviv")

Due to the immigration of Jewish architects, architecture has come to reflect different styles. In the early 20th century Jewish architects sought to combine Occidental and Oriental architecture producing buildings that showcase a myriad of infused styles.[[703]](#cite_note-712) The [eclectic](/wiki/Eclecticism_in_architecture "Eclecticism in architecture") style gave way to the modernist [Bauhaus](/wiki/Bauhaus "Bauhaus") style with the influx of German Jewish architects (among them [Erich Mendelsohn](/wiki/Erich_Mendelsohn "Erich Mendelsohn")) fleeing [Nazi persecution](/wiki/Nazi_persecution_of_Jews "Nazi persecution of Jews").[[704]](#cite_note-713)[[705]](#cite_note-714) The [White City of Tel Aviv](/wiki/White_City,_Tel_Aviv "White City, Tel Aviv") is a [UNESCO heritage site](/wiki/UNESCO_Heritage_site "UNESCO Heritage site").[[706]](#cite_note-715) Following independence, multiple government projects were commissioned, a grand part built in a brutalist style with heavy emphasis on the use of concrete and acclimatisation to the desert climate.[[707]](#cite_note-716)[[708]](#cite_note-717)

Several novel ideas such as the [Garden City](/wiki/Garden_city_movement "Garden city movement") were implemented in Israeli cities; the [Geddes plan](/wiki/Geddes_Plan_for_Tel_Aviv "Geddes Plan for Tel Aviv") of Tel Aviv became renowned internationally for its revolutionary design and adaptation to the local climate.[[709]](#cite_note-718) The design of kibbutzim also came to reflect ideology, such as the planning of the circular kibbutz [Nahalal](/wiki/Nahalal "Nahalal") by [Richard Kauffmann](/wiki/Richard_Kauffmann "Richard Kauffmann").[[710]](#cite_note-719)

### Museums

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of Israeli museums](/wiki/List_of_Israeli_museums "List of Israeli museums").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Billy_Rose_Art_Garden_%2814755133799%29.jpg/250px-Billy_Rose_Art_Garden_%2814755133799%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Billy_Rose_Art_Garden_(14755133799).jpg)

[Shrine of the Book](/wiki/Shrine_of_the_Book "Shrine of the Book"), repository of the [Dead Sea Scrolls](/wiki/Dead_Sea_Scrolls "Dead Sea Scrolls") in Jerusalem

The [Israel Museum](/wiki/Israel_Museum "Israel Museum") in Jerusalem is one of Israel's most important cultural institutions,[[711]](#cite_note-imj-720) and houses the [Dead Sea Scrolls](/wiki/Dead_Sea_Scrolls "Dead Sea Scrolls"),[[712]](#cite_note-721) along with an extensive collection of [Judaica](/wiki/Judaica "Judaica") and [European art](/wiki/European_art "European art").[[711]](#cite_note-imj-720) [Yad Vashem](/wiki/Yad_Vashem "Yad Vashem") ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): יָד וַשֵׁם, lit. 'a memorial and a name') is the world's central [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust") memorial institution and archive of Holocaust-related information.[[713]](#cite_note-722) [ANU - Museum of the Jewish People](/wiki/ANU_-_Museum_of_the_Jewish_People "ANU - Museum of the Jewish People") is an interactive museum devoted to the history of Jewish communities around the world.[[714]](#cite_note-723)

Israel has the highest number of museums per capita.[[715]](#cite_note-724) Several museums are devoted to Islamic culture, including the [Rockefeller Archeological Museum](/wiki/Rockefeller_Museum "Rockefeller Museum") and the [L. A. Mayer Institute for Islamic Art](/wiki/L._A._Mayer_Institute_for_Islamic_Art "L. A. Mayer Institute for Islamic Art"), both in Jerusalem. The Rockefeller specialises in archaeological remains from Middle East history. It is also the home of the first hominid fossil skull found in West Asia, called [Galilee Man](/wiki/Galilee_Man "Galilee Man").[[716]](#cite_note-725)

### Mass media

Main article: [Mass media in Israel](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Israel "Mass media in Israel")

Media is diverse. Notable newspapers include the left-wing *[Haaretz](/wiki/Haaretz "Haaretz")*,[[717]](#cite_note-726) centrist *[Yedioth Ahronoth](/wiki/Yedioth_Ahronoth "Yedioth Ahronoth")*,[[718]](#cite_note-727) and center-right *[Israel Hayom](/wiki/Israel_Hayom "Israel Hayom")*.[[719]](#cite_note-728) There are several major television channels which cater to different audiences, from Russian-language [Channel 9](/wiki/Israel_Plus "Israel Plus")[[720]](#cite_note-729) to Arabic-language [Kan 33](/wiki/Kan_33 "Kan 33").[[721]](#cite_note-730) The 2024 Freedom House report found Israeli media is "vibrant and free to criticise government policy".[[722]](#cite_note-731) In the 2024 [Press Freedom Index](/wiki/Press_Freedom_Index "Press Freedom Index") by [Reporters Without Borders](/wiki/Reporters_Without_Borders "Reporters Without Borders"), Israel was placed 112th of 180 countries, second in the Middle East and North Africa.[[723]](#cite_note-732)[[724]](#cite_note-RSFNAME-733)

Reporters Without Borders reported that the Israel Defence Forces had killed more than 100 Palestinian journalists in Gaza. During the 2023 Gaza war, Israel has been alleged to be "trying to suppress the reporting coming out of the besieged enclave while disinformation infiltrates its own media ecosystem".[[724]](#cite_note-RSFNAME-733) In May 2024, Israel shut down the local offices of [Al Jazeera](/wiki/Al_Jazeera "Al Jazeera").[[725]](#cite_note-734) In 2024, according to the [Committee to Protect Journalists](/wiki/Committee_to_Protect_Journalists "Committee to Protect Journalists"), Israel was the second leading country in jailing journalists,[[726]](#cite_note-735) and responsible for the majority of journalists killed in the world.[[727]](#cite_note-736)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Israeli cuisine](/wiki/Israeli_cuisine "Israeli cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Food_in_Israel.jpg/250px-Food_in_Israel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Food_in_Israel.jpg)

A meal including [falafel](/wiki/Falafel "Falafel"), [hummus](/wiki/Hummus "Hummus"), [French fries](/wiki/French_fries "French fries") and [Israeli salad](/wiki/Israeli_salad "Israeli salad")

Israeli cuisine includes local dishes as well as [Jewish cuisine](/wiki/Jewish_cuisine "Jewish cuisine") brought to the country by immigrants. Particularly since the late 1970s, a [fusion cuisine](/wiki/Fusion_cuisine "Fusion cuisine") has developed.[[728]](#cite_note-raviv-737) The cuisine has adapted elements of the [Mizrahi](/wiki/Mizrahi_Jewish_cuisine "Mizrahi Jewish cuisine"), [Sephardi](/wiki/Sephardic_Jewish_cuisine "Sephardic Jewish cuisine"), and [Ashkenazi](/wiki/Ashkenazi_cuisine "Ashkenazi cuisine") styles of cooking. It incorporates many foods traditionally eaten in the [Levantine](/wiki/Levantine_cuisine "Levantine cuisine"), [Arab](/wiki/Arab_cuisine "Arab cuisine"), [Middle Eastern](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_cuisine "Middle Eastern cuisine") and [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_cuisine "Mediterranean cuisine") cuisines, such as [falafel](/wiki/Falafel "Falafel"), [hummus](/wiki/Hummus "Hummus"), [shakshouka](/wiki/Shakshouka "Shakshouka"), [couscous](/wiki/Couscous "Couscous"), and [za'atar](/wiki/Za%27atar "Za'atar"). [Schnitzel](/wiki/Schnitzel "Schnitzel"), [pizza](/wiki/Pizza "Pizza"), [hamburgers](/wiki/Hamburger "Hamburger"), [French fries](/wiki/French_fries "French fries"), [rice](/wiki/Rice "Rice") and [salad](/wiki/Salad "Salad") are common. [Ptitim](/wiki/Israeli_couscous "Israeli couscous") (Israeli couscous) is a notable Israeli food invented in the 1950s due to rice shortages during the [austerity period](/wiki/Austerity_in_Israel "Austerity in Israel").[[729]](#cite_note-JannaGur-738)

Roughly half of the Jewish population attests to keeping [kosher](/wiki/Kosher "Kosher") at home.[[730]](#cite_note-739)[[731]](#cite_note-bernstein_227,_233–234-740) [Kosher restaurants](/wiki/Kosher_restaurant "Kosher restaurant") make up around a quarter of the total as of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Israel&action=edit).[[728]](#cite_note-raviv-737) Pork—often called "white meat" in Israel[[732]](#cite_note-bernstein-231-741)—is produced and consumed despite attempts to ban it; [it is forbidden](/wiki/Religious_restrictions_on_the_consumption_of_pork "Religious restrictions on the consumption of pork") by both Judaism and Islam but is permitted by Christianity and mostly produced in traditionally Christian areas of northern Israel.[[733]](#cite_note-porkprod-742)[[734]](#cite_note-743) Other non-kosher foods produced and eaten in Israel include rabbits, ostriches, and non-kosher fish.[[732]](#cite_note-bernstein-231-741)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Israel](/wiki/Sport_in_Israel "Sport in Israel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/%D7%99%D7%A8%D7%95%D7%A7_%D7%A2%D7%95%D7%9C%D7%94.jpg/250px-%D7%99%D7%A8%D7%95%D7%A7_%D7%A2%D7%95%D7%9C%D7%94.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D7%99%D7%A8%D7%95%D7%A7_%D7%A2%D7%95%D7%9C%D7%94.jpg)

[Maccabi Haifa F.C.](/wiki/Maccabi_Haifa_F.C. "Maccabi Haifa F.C.") fans at [Sammy Ofer Stadium](/wiki/Sammy_Ofer_Stadium "Sammy Ofer Stadium") in the city of Haifa

The most popular spectator sports in Israel are association football and basketball.[[735]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorstrick2004141-744) The [Israeli Premier League](/wiki/Israeli_Premier_League "Israeli Premier League") is the country's premier football league, and the [Israeli Basketball Premier League](/wiki/Israeli_Basketball_Premier_League "Israeli Basketball Premier League") is the premier basketball league.[[736]](#cite_note-745) [Maccabi Haifa](/wiki/Maccabi_Haifa_F.C. "Maccabi Haifa F.C."), [Maccabi Tel Aviv](/wiki/Maccabi_Tel_Aviv_F.C. "Maccabi Tel Aviv F.C."), [Hapoel Tel Aviv](/wiki/Hapoel_Tel_Aviv_F.C. "Hapoel Tel Aviv F.C.") and [Beitar Jerusalem](/wiki/Beitar_Jerusalem_F.C. "Beitar Jerusalem F.C.") are the largest [football clubs](/wiki/List_of_football_clubs_in_Israel "List of football clubs in Israel"). Maccabi Tel Aviv, Maccabi Haifa and Hapoel Tel Aviv have competed in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") and Hapoel Tel Aviv reached the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") quarter-finals. Israel hosted and won the [1964 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/1964_AFC_Asian_Cup "1964 AFC Asian Cup"); in 1970 the [Israel national football team](/wiki/Israel_national_football_team "Israel national football team") qualified for the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1970_FIFA_World_Cup "1970 FIFA World Cup"), the only time it participated. The [1974 Asian Games](/wiki/1974_Asian_Games "1974 Asian Games"), held in Tehran, were the last Asian Games in which Israel [participated](/wiki/Israel_at_the_Asian_Games "Israel at the Asian Games"), plagued by Arab countries that [refused to compete](/wiki/Boycotts_of_Israel_in_sports "Boycotts of Israel in sports") with Israel. Israel was excluded from the [1978 Asian Games](/wiki/1978_Asian_Games "1978 Asian Games") and since then has not competed in Asian sport events.[[737]](#cite_note-746) In 1994, [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") agreed to admit Israel, and its football teams now compete in Europe. [Maccabi Tel Aviv B.C.](/wiki/Maccabi_Tel_Aviv_B.C. "Maccabi Tel Aviv B.C.") has won the [European championship](/wiki/FIBA_European_Champions_Cup_and_EuroLeague_records_and_statistics "FIBA European Champions Cup and EuroLeague records and statistics") in basketball six times.[[738]](#cite_note-747)

Israel has won [20 Olympic medals](/wiki/Israel_at_the_Olympics "Israel at the Olympics") since its first win [in 1992](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics"), including a gold medal in [windsurfing](/wiki/Sailing_at_the_2004_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_Mistral_One_Design "Sailing at the 2004 Summer Olympics – Men's Mistral One Design") at the [2004 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2004_Summer_Olympics "2004 Summer Olympics"),[[739]](#cite_note-748) and seven medals at the 2024 Paris Olympics alone.[[740]](#cite_note-749) Israel has won [over 100](/wiki/Israel_at_the_Paralympics "Israel at the Paralympics") gold medals in the [Paralympic Games](/wiki/Paralympic_Games "Paralympic Games") and is ranked 20th in the [all-time medal count](/wiki/All-time_Paralympic_Games_medal_table "All-time Paralympic Games medal table"). The [1968 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/1968_Summer_Paralympics "1968 Summer Paralympics") were hosted by Israel.[[741]](#cite_note-750) The [Maccabiah Games](/wiki/Maccabiah_Games "Maccabiah Games"), an Olympic-style event for [Jewish](/wiki/List_of_Jews_in_sports "List of Jews in sports") and Israeli athletes, was inaugurated in the 1930s, and has been held every four years since. [Krav Maga](/wiki/Krav_Maga "Krav Maga"), a martial art developed by Jewish ghetto defenders, is used by the Israeli security forces and police.[[742]](#cite_note-751)

[Chess](/wiki/Chess "Chess") is a leading sport. There are many Israeli grandmasters and [Israeli chess players](/wiki/List_of_Israeli_chess_players "List of Israeli chess players") have won a number of youth world championships.[[743]](#cite_note-752) Israel stages an annual international [championship](/wiki/Israeli_Chess_Championship "Israeli Chess Championship") and hosted the [World Team Chess Championship](/wiki/World_Team_Chess_Championship "World Team Chess Championship") in 2005.

[S2CID](/wiki/S2CID_(identifier) "S2CID (identifier)") [150208821](https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusID:150208821). "The mass immigration from Arab countries began in mid-1949 and included three communities that relocated to Israel almost in their entirety: 31,000 Jews from Libya, 50,000 from Yemen, and 125,000 from Iraq. Additional immigrants arrived from Egypt, Morocco, Tunisia, Turkey, Iran, India, and elsewhere. Within three years, the Jewish population of Israel doubled. The ethnic composition of the population shifted as well, as immigrants from Muslim counties and their offspring now comprised one third of the Jewish population—an unprecedented phenomenon in global immigration history. From 1952–60, Israel regulated and restricted immigration from Muslim countries with a selective immigration policy based on economic criteria, and sent these immigrants, most of whom were North African, to peripheral Israeli settlements. The selective immigration policy ended in 1961 when, following an agreement between Israel and Morocco, about 100,000 Jews immigrated to the State. From 1952–68 about 600,000 Jews arrived in Israel, three quarters of whom were from Arab countries and the remaining immigrants were largely from Eastern Europe. Today fewer than 30,000 remain in Muslim countries, mostly concentrated in Iran and Turkey."

104. **[^](#cite_ref-113)** Raviv, Dvir; Ben David, Chaim (2021). ["Cassius Dio's figures for the demographic consequences of the Bar Kokhba War: Exaggeration or reliable account?"](https://doi.org/10.1017%2FS1047759421000271). *Journal of Roman Archaeology*. **34** (2): 585–607. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1017/S1047759421000271](https://doi.org/10.1017%2FS1047759421000271). [S2CID](/wiki/S2CID_(identifier) "S2CID (identifier)") [245512193](https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusID:245512193). "Scholars have long doubted the historical accuracy of Cassius Dio's account of the consequences of the Bar Kokhba War (Roman History 69.14). According to this text, considered the most reliable literary source for the Second Jewish Revolt, the war encompassed all of Judea: the Romans destroyed 985 villages and 50 fortresses, and killed 580,000 rebels. This article reassesses Cassius Dio's figures by drawing on new evidence from excavations and surveys in Judea, Transjordan, and the Galilee. Three research methods are combined: an ethno-archaeological comparison with the settlement picture in the Ottoman Period, comparison with similar settlement studies in the Galilee, and an evaluation of settled sites from the Middle Roman Period (70–136 CE). The study demonstrates the potential contribution of the archaeological record to this issue and supports the view of Cassius Dio's demographic data as a reliable account, which he based on contemporaneous documentation."
105. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-:02_114-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-:02_114-1) Mor, Menahem (18 April 2016). *The Second Jewish Revolt*. BRILL. pp. 483–484. "Land confiscation in Judaea was part of the suppression of the revolt policy of the Romans and punishment for the rebels. But the very claim that the sikarikon laws were annulled for settlement purposes seems to indicate that Jews continued to reside in Judaea even after the Second Revolt. There is no doubt that this area suffered the severest damage from the suppression of the revolt. Settlements in Judaea, such as Herodion and Bethar, had already been destroyed during the course of the revolt, and Jews were expelled from the districts of Gophna, Herodion, and Aqraba. However, it should not be claimed that the region of Judaea was completely destroyed. Jews continued to live in areas such as Lod (Lydda), south of the Hebron Mountain, and the coastal regions. In other areas of the Land of Israel that did not have any direct connection with the Second Revolt, no settlement changes can be identified as resulting from it."

[OCLC](/wiki/OCLC_(identifier) "OCLC (identifier)") [1302180905](https://search.worldcat.org/oclc/1302180905). "The Jewish community strove to recover from the catastrophic results of the Bar Kokhva revolt (132–135 CE). Although some of these attempts were relatively successful, the Jews never fully recovered. During the Late Roman and Byzantine periods, many Jews emigrated to thriving centres in the diaspora, especially Iraq, whereas some converted to Christianity and others continued to live in the Holy Land, especially in Galilee and the coastal plain. During the Byzantine period, the three provinces of Palestine included more than thirty cities, namely, settlements with a bishop see. After the Muslim conquest in the 630s, most of these cities declined and eventually disappeared. As a result, in many cases the local ecclesiastical administration weakened, while in others it simply ceased to exist. Consequently, many local Christians converted to Islam. Thus, almost twelve centuries later, when the army led by Napoleon Bonaparte arrived in the Holy Land, most of the local population was Muslim."

     * [Imseis 2021](#CITEREFImseis2021), pp. 13–14: "As to territorial boundaries, under the plan the Jewish State was allotted approximately 57 per cent of the total area of Palestine even though the Jewish population comprised only 33 per cent of the country. In addition, according to British records relied upon by the ad hoc committee, the Jewish population possessed registered ownership of only 5.6 per cent of Palestine, and was eclipsed by the Arabs in land ownership in every one of Palestine's 16 sub-districts. Moreover, the quality of the land granted to the proposed Jewish state was highly skewed in its favour. UNSCOP reported that under its majority plan "[t]he Jews will have the more economically developed part of the country embracing practically the whole of the citrus-producing area"—Palestine's staple export crop—even though approximately half of the citrus-bearing land was owned by the Arabs. In addition, according to updated British records submitted to the ad hoc committee's two sub-committees, "of the irrigated, cultivable areas" of the country, 84 per cent would be in the Jewish State and 16 per cent would be in the Arab State"."

"The escalation continued with the Egyptian blockade of the Straits of Tiran, and Nasser's nationalization of the Suez Canal in July 1956. On October 14, Nasser made clear his intent:"I am not solely fighting against Israel itself. My task is to deliver the Arab world from destruction through Israel's intrigue, which has its roots abroad. Our hatred is very strong. There is no sense in talking about peace with Israel. There is not even the smallest place for negotiations." Less than two weeks later, on October 25, Egypt signed a tripartite agreement with Syria and Jordan placing Nasser in command of all three armies. The continued blockade of the Suez Canal and Gulf of Aqaba to Israeli shipping, combined with the increased fedayeen attacks and the bellicosity of recent Arab statements, prompted Israel, with the backing of Britain and France, to attack Egypt on October 29, 1956."

352. **[^](#cite_ref-361)** Charbit, Denis (2014). ["Israel's Self-Restrained Secularism from the 1947 Status Quo Letter to the Present"](https://books.google.com/books?id=gThvBAAAQBAJ&pg=PAPA167). In Berlinerblau, Jacques; Fainberg, Sarah; Nou, Aurora (eds.). *Secularism on the Edge: Rethinking Church-State Relations in the United States, France, and Israel*. Palgrave Macmillan. pp. 167–169. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-1-137-38115-6](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-137-38115-6 "Special:BookSources/978-1-137-38115-6"). "The compromise, therefore, was to choose constructive ambiguity: as surprising as it may seem, there is no law that declares Judaism the official religion of Israel. However, there is no other law that declares Israel's neutrality toward all confessions. Judaism is not recognized as the official religion of the state, and even though the Jewish, Muslim and Christian clergy receive their salaries from the state, this fact does not make Israel a neutral state. This apparent pluralism cannot dissimulate the fact that Israel displays a clear and undoubtedly hierarchical pluralism in religious matters. ... It is important to note that from a multicultural point of view, this self-restrained secularism allows Muslim law to be practiced in Israel for personal matters of the Muslim community. As surprising as it seems, if not paradoxical for a state in war, Israel is the only Western democratic country in which Sharia enjoys such an official status."
353. **[^](#cite_ref-362)** Sharot, Stephen (2007). ["Judaism in Israel: Public Religion, Neo-Traditionalism, Messianism, and Ethno-Religious Conflict"](https://books.google.com/books?id=vA8edg7bv0kC&pg=PAPA671). In Beckford, James A.; Demerath, Jay (eds.). *The Sage Handbook of the Sociology of Religion*. Sage Publications. pp. 671–672. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-1-4129-1195-5](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-4129-1195-5 "Special:BookSources/978-1-4129-1195-5"). "It is true that Jewish Israelis, and secular Israelis in particular, conceive of religion as shaped by a state-sponsored religious establishment. There is no formal state religion in Israel, but the state gives its official recognition and financial support to particular religious communities, Jewish, Islamic and Christian, whose religious authorities and courts are empowered to deal with matters of personal status and family law, such as marriage, divorce, and alimony, that are binding on all members of the communities."
354. **[^](#cite_ref-363)** Jacoby, Tami Amanda (2005). [*Women in Zones of Conflict: Power and Resistance in Israel*](https://books.google.com/books?id=pr1LJNrlmuIC&pg=PAPA53). McGill-Queen's University Press. pp. 53–54. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-7735-2993-9](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-7735-2993-9 "Special:BookSources/978-0-7735-2993-9"). "Although there is no official religion in Israel, there is also no clear separation between religion and state. In Israeli public life, tensions frequently arise among different streams of Judaism: Ultra-Orthodox, National-Religious, *Mesorati* (Conservative), Reconstructionist Progressive (Reform), and varying combinations of traditionalism and non-observance. Despite this variety in religious observances in society, Orthodox Judaism prevails institutionally over the other streams. This boundary is an historical consequence of the unique evolution of the relationship between Israel nationalism and state building. ... Since the founding period, in order to defuse religious tensions, the State of Israel has adopted what is known as the 'status quo,' an unwritten agreement stipulating that no further changes would be made in the status of religion, and that conflict between the observant and non-observant sectors would be handled circumstantially. The 'status quo' has since pertained to the legal status of both religious and secular Jews in Israel. This situation was designed to appease the religious sector, and has been upheld indefinitely through the disproportionate power of religious political parties in all subsequent coalition governments. ... On one hand, the Declaration of Independence adopted in 1948 explicitly guarantees freedom of religion. On the other, it simultaneously prevents the separation of religion and state in Israel."
